Â Â 

(Fine della procedura "catch the eye")

Â 
Â Â 
Â Â 

VORSITZ: SABINE VERHEYEN
VizeprÃ¤sidentin

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â MaroÅ¡ Å efÄoviÄ, Member of the Commission. â Madam President, Madam Minister, honourable Members, thank you very much for the debate and also for your passion for Europe, which was very clear from your interventions. They reflected the wide range of topics to be discussed at the next European Council by the European leaders.

So let me just react on a few most recurring issues and points you've been raising in your speeches.

First, I would like to reassure you how important the competitiveness for the European Commission is. And I think the last intervention on this was Madam Grapini, and we are working together very well on the Customs Union reform, and therefore I am using this opportunity to underscore that also, the President, not only yesterday when we have been presenting the Commission work programme, but also this morning elaborated in great detail on a clean tech, on the importance of the industry for Europe, but also on the crucial role we all have to play in cutting red tape for our economic operators.

And therefore, I just would like to highlight one additional aspect which we, I think covered more yesterday than today. And this is our focus on farmers, fishers and agrifood sector.

My colleague, Commissioner for Agriculture Christophe Hansen, together with the whole college, is working very hard on implementing the new strategic vision for agriculture. We are looking from every aspect how to further minimise reporting obligations and how to motivate young people to become farmers. It was the proposal the Commission put on the table just yesterday. You would agree with me that the European Union is an agri-food superpower, and we will do everything possible to keep it that way also in the future.

I also would like to thank honourable Members for the focus of our debate today on the need to address jointly energy prices, upgrade of our energy grids and to tackle the bureaucracy on all levels. Mr Nica was referring to energy prices and my compatriot, Mr HojsÃ­k, Vice-President of the Parliament, was focusing on the importance of the joint combined effort. We have to do our utmost if it comes to simplification from the EU level. But of course the Member States have to help us even more than today to stop the bureaucratic gold plating on the national and regional level.

Mario Draghi was right. If we can get rid of national barriers, if we can remove the obstacles on our single market, it would more than compensate for the recent tariff barriers being erected on the global trade level.

Despite of all this, I would like to reassure the honourable Members and this House that we are working very hard to bring to our economic operators, farmers, industry, SMEs, new market access new business opportunities through our ambitious agenda of free trade agreements. We want to make sure that we would help to secure our independence by introducing, in all our agreements, very ambitious chapters on the critical raw materials, and we are extending a hand to all our partners, be it under global gateway projects or other agreements which we are proposing to them to work on mutually advantageous projects that would create jointly value added in those countries and provide the necessary critical raw material supplies also for the European economy.

Mr Van Lanschot, under the catch-the-eye procedure, was rightly referring to the fact that housing is now new focus for all the interinstitutional cooperation, and we in the European Commission are very happy about that. That it's a priority for European Parliament, it's a priority for the Council. The leaders under the European Council setting will be discussing exactly the issue, which is so problematic for young people across Europe. And I believe that this political concentration and attention given to this topic will bring us also concrete results.

And I would like also to react on one of the latest interventions by Ms TemidoÂ and Mr Oliveira on poverty. I think just yesterday we had a full debate on the Commission work programme, and I am sure that you will see that if it comes to anti-poverty strategy, if you would look for enhanced child guarantee, it is all there. And we know how important it is for our youth, how important it is for people of Europe. And I believe that jointly with you and with the Council, we will deliver on this very important priority.

And to conclude, Madam President, I would just like to thank the honourable Members for the debate, and I am sure that the questions related to defence, security and foreign policy will be covered by my esteemed colleague, Minister Bjerre, as it is in accordance with our Treaties, and therefore, I just would like to thank everyone for the discussions, for the questions, and I believe, for support of the very ambitious agenda for the future of Europe.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Marie Bjerre, President-in-Office of the Council. â MadamÂ President, honourable Members, Commissioner, I want to thank you for your remarks and comments. Let me give a few remarks on the debate.

Many of you referred to Ukraine. I want to say that is also a very, very important agenda for the Council. We need to further increase our pressure on Russia. We have to keep supporting Ukraine and we have to keep up the EU accession plan for Ukraine. Also, it is of utmost importance that we ramp up Europe's defence capacities and that we support the roadmap on defence readiness.

Many of you also touched upon the green transition and competitiveness, and I think the debate illustrates quite well that green transition and competitiveness goes hand in hand. We have been leading in many green technologies in Europe and green technologies are the future. But if we don't make the right balances and if we don't have the right means in reaching our climate goals, then we risk overwhelming our businesses with burdens. That is why both simplification and the green transition are important topics for the leaders tomorrow.

Also, several of you touched upon housing. It is clear from the debate that housing is an important topic. It is also for the Council. It has become a growing social and economic concern for many Member States. While respecting the national competences, there is still scope for cooperation and that is why it is now on the agenda. It's on the agenda for the first time and the Danish Presidency aims to reach Council conclusions in December on this matter.

Many other important topics were also brought up in this debate, for instance migration. You all know that is also a key topic for the Danish Presidency. I think all these topics mentioned today also show that it is a quite substantive agenda the leaders have tomorrow. But there are also many, many challenges that Europe is faced with and we need to solve them together.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â President. â The debate is closed.

Schriftliche ErklÃ¤rungen (Artikel 178)

Â 
Â Â 
Â 		Â 

Â Â Susana SolÃ­s PÃ©rez (PPE), por escrito. â Europa vive un tiempo de decisiones que marcarÃ¡n su destino. El prÃ³ximo Consejo Europeo debe dar respuestas claras a los grandes retos que ponen a prueba nuestra uniÃ³n: la defensa, la competitividad, la gestiÃ³n de la migraciÃ³n y el futuro de nuestros jÃ³venes.

Europa no puede limitarse a observar mientras Rusia mantiene su agresiÃ³n a Ucrania y Trump y Xi redefinen el orden mundial. Debemos hablar con una sola voz, garantizar el apoyo financiero y militar a Ucrania y avanzar hacia una verdadera defensa europea.

La pÃ©rdida de empleo industrial y el estancamiento del mercado interior exigen una nueva estrategia de crecimiento que combine ambiciÃ³n climÃ¡tica y sentido comÃºn. La transiciÃ³n ecolÃ³gica no puede hacerse a costa del empleo ni de la competitividad de nuestras empresas.

TambiÃ©n necesitamos una polÃ­tica migratoria comÃºn, firme y humana. Europa debe proteger sus fronteras, desmantelar las redes que trafican con personas y abrir vÃ­as legales y seguras para quienes buscan una vida mejor.

Finalmente, debemos ofrecer esperanza a los jÃ³venes: acceso a la vivienda, oportunidades laborales y un proyecto de vida en el continente que construyeron Adenauer, De Gasperi y Schuman.

El Consejo debe estar a la altura de este momento histÃ³rico. No es tiempo de discursos, sino de acciÃ³n y unidad.

Â 

4. Situation in Belarus, five years after the fraudulent presidential elections (debate)
Video of the speeches
Â 

Â Â Die PrÃ¤sidentin. â Als nÃ¤chster Punkt der Tagesordnung folgt die Aussprache Ã¼ber die ErklÃ¤rung der Kommission zur Lage in Belarus fÃ¼nf Jahre nach der manipulierten PrÃ¤sidentschaftswahl (2025/2900(RSP)).

Ich mÃ¶chte Sie nochmals daran erinnern, dass es auch bei dieser Aussprache keine vorherige Rednerliste geben wird.

Nach der ersten Runde der Fraktionen werden die Mitglieder, die von ihren Fraktionen oder vom Sekretariat fÃ¼r fraktionslose Mitglieder Redezeit erhalten haben, aufgerufen, das Wort zu ergreifen. An die Ihnen zugeteilte Redezeit mÃ¶chte ich auch noch einmal erinnern, weil wir um 12.00 Uhr einen Gast hier erwarten und deshalb auch pÃ¼nktlich die Debatte beenden mÃ¼ssen.

Bitte beachten Sie die Bildschirme im Plenarsaal, auf denen der aktuelle Redner sowie die drei nachfolgenden Redner angezeigt werden. Sobald Ihr Name als nÃ¤chster Redner oder nÃ¤chste Rednerin angezeigt wird, bereiten Sie sich bitte vor und begeben Sie sich zum Rednerpult. WÃ¤hrend der Aussprache gelten die Ã¼blichen Regeln fÃ¼r blaue Karten und spontane Wortmeldungen.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Dan JÃ¸rgensen, Member of the Commission. â Madam President, distinguished Members of the European Parliament, this August, we commemorated the fifth anniversary of the 2020 elections in Belarus, which were a source of enormous hope for the Belarusian people. Sadly, we all know how the regime responded to this peaceful call for change.

Five years on from 9Â AugustÂ 2020, Lukashenka has chosen the path of accelerating repression over that of engagement and reform. The recent release of political prisoners, including prominent Belarusian activists, journalists, as well as EU nationals, is a positive step. We are especially relieved that our colleagues from the EU Delegation in Minsk were amongst those released. Since 2020 and before, supporting the victims of repression in Belarus has been at the core of our policy. Our victims facility has provided relocation, legal, medical and psychological support to victims of repression and political prisoners in more than 7Â 500 cases. I can say today that we are already providing support to the majority of those released in September. If further releases take place, the EU will be there to support those who need it.

Despite the good news, we cannot ignore the harsh realities. Over 1Â 200 individuals remain arbitrarily detained for political reasons. This is an increase compared to when we last spoke about Belarus here in July. Included in that number is Andrzej Poczobut, who you have honoured as a nominee for this year's Sakharov Prize. Thank you for highlighting this and other cases in your resolutions and declarations over the last year.Â The case of political prisoner Mikalai Statkevich is particularly concerning. After refusing to be removed from Belarus in the release on 11Â September, his whereabouts are unknown and a group of UN experts have warned he could be the victim of enforced disappearance. We call on Belarusian authorities to immediately make clear where he is being held and, ultimately, to release him without enforcing his deportation from the Belarus.

The EU condemns Belarus's continued support to Russia's illegal war of aggression against Ukraine. Recently, this has involved supplying oil and gas to supplement Russia's energy supply. Since 2021, Belarus has instrumental migration flows as a political tool to pressure the EU's external borders. The EU stands in solidarity with the affected Member States.

In July, in this House, I made clear what we expect to see from the Belarusian authorities. We call for the immediate and unconditional release of all political prisoners and end of repression. The Belarusian regime must put an end to arbitrary detentions, halt the creation and enforcement of repressive laws and stop politically motivated prosecutions, including trials heldÂ in absentia. We call on the regime to respect the rights of the detainees, in line with Belarus's international obligations. Belarusian authorities must also stop incommunicado detentions and ill treatment of prisoners.

Until we see a fundamental shift in these areas, we will continue to apply pressure on the regime while supporting the people of Belarus and their democratic aspirations. The EU has imposed several packages of restrictive measures, both sectoral and individual, targeting those responsible for repression and human rights abuses, misconduct of the election process, complicity in the war against Ukraine and inciting and organising illegal border crossings.

The EU is also leading accountability efforts at the regional and international level, and supporting accountability mechanisms like the International Accountability Platform for Belarus. At the same time, the EU continues to stand in solidarity with the people of Belarus. We have already mobilised EURÂ 170 million to support the people. We will continue to support Belarusians, independent media, civil society and those committed to building a peaceful and democratic future.

We are ready to work with you, the European Parliament, our Member States and international partners to support the people of Belarus. We are in this for the long haul and we hope we can count on your support in this endeavour.

Â 
Â Â 
Â

Miejsce Alaksandra Åukaszenki jest przed MiÄdzynarodowym TrybunaÅem Karnym. On tam powinien odpowiadaÄ, a nie szukaÄ miejsca na politycznych salonach. Â Â Pierfrancesco Maran, a nome del gruppo S&D. â Signora Presidente, onorevoli colleghi, in Bielorussia vige un regime, quello di Lukashenko, che Ã¨ anche complice della guerra in Ucraina. Ma c'Ã¨ chi lotta per un futuro diverso: Sergey Tsichanouski Ã¨ stato incarcerato perchÃ© si Ã¨ candidato alle elezioni del 2020; sua moglie Sviatlana Tsichanouskaya si Ã¨ candidata al suo posto e, nonostante le intimidazioni e le minacce, ha eroicamente costituito un movimento di opposizione al regime, per poi essere costretta all'esilio.

Noi, Europa, dobbiamo chiederci cosa possiamo fare di concreto per chi lotta per la libertÃ  in Bielorussia E continueremo a batterci per la liberazione dei prigionieri politici e per chiedere elezioni libere. Lo faremo continuando a sostenere le voci dei media indipendenti e della societÃ  civile che resiste. Lo faremo chiedendo ai paesi europei di essere al fianco dei rifugiati politici bielorussi anche nella vita quotidiana, a cominciare dall'agevolazione nel rinnovo dei visti.

Noi europei amiamo la libertÃ  e chi lotta per essa: per questo vogliamo che bielorussi siano liberi di poter decidere del loro futuro.

Wolna BiaÅoruÅ to nie tylko geopolityka, to sprawa sumienia.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Sergey Lagodinsky, on behalf of the Verts/ALE Group. â Madam President, five years ago, people in Belarus took to the streets. They carried no weapons, no hate in their hearts, only flowers. I remember the phone calls from Minsk. The talks with activists. Their courage, without violence, without blood.

Today, they are in prisons or they're dead, like Maria Zaitseva, Ales Pushkin or Ihar Lednik. Today, Belarus is not a sovereign country; it's a Russian military base from which rockets are flying to Ukraine.

But Belarus is also something else. It is a democratic movement with a clear, courageous face: Sviatlana Tsikhanouskaya. Sviatlana and her team have managed to act professionally, clearly and politically as a voice for Belarus. Our task is clear: not to betray this movement, not to turn our backs on them like the German Government is currently doing by denying visas to those who look for protection. If you refuse to see clearly, then we will. We will make Belarus visible, as people of freedom. We will make its heroes political prisoners visible. And we will give Belarusians what Moscow wants to take away from them: hope for a European home. Zhyve Belarus!

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Danilo Della Valle, a nome del gruppo The Left. â Signora Presidente, signor Commissario, onorevoli colleghi, oggi parliamo di Bielorussia e voglio premettere che Lukashenko Ã¨ l'espressione piÃ¹ lontana dalla mia idea di politica.

PerÃ² mi dÃ  l'occasione per ribadire anche le storture di questo Parlamento, perchÃ© nella risoluzione di oggi, ad esempio, si dice che questo Parlamento non riconosce come legittima la presidenza Bielorussa. E questo non Ã¨ l'unico caso: succede con il Venezuela, con Cuba, con la Georgia e potrei continuare a lungo. Succede ovunque ci sono governi non allineati alla politica estera di Bruxelles.

Giustamente parlate di difesa dei diritti umani: ma spesso vi nascondete dietro questa difesa, perchÃ© non mi pare che dedichiate lo stesso approccio quando si parla di Arabia Saudita, Qatar, Israele, dove le vite dei palestinesi valgono meno di quelle dei vostri animali da compagnia.

State condannando l'Europa all'irrilevanza di fronte alla storia. Credete che a qualcuno dei 7 miliardi di esseri umani che vivono al di fuori della bolla dell'Occidente interessi cosa riconosce o non riconosce il Parlamento europeo? Pensate che queste cose possano davvero migliorare la vita di bielorussi, venezuelani, georgiani? Che credibilitÃ  hanno le vostre accuse, se sono mosse da doppi standard e da calcoli geopolitici?

L'unica strada per tutti noi Ã¨ guardare in faccia alla realtÃ : lavorare per un mondo multipolare, di dialogo, di cooperazione e di pace.

Â 
Â Â 
Â

ÐÐµÐ¹ÑÑÐ²Ð¸ÑÐµÐ»Ð½Ð¾ÑÑÑÐ° Ðµ Ð´Ð¾ÐºÐ°Ð·Ð°Ð»Ð°, ÑÐµ ÑÐ¾Ð¼ Ð£ÑÑÑÐ»Ð° ÑÐ¾Ð½ Ð´ÐµÑ ÐÐ°Ð¹ÐµÐ½ Ð½Ð¸ ÐºÑÐ¸ÑÐ¸ÐºÑÐ²Ð°, Ð·Ð½Ð°ÑÐ¸ ÑÐ¼Ðµ Ð½Ð° Ð¿ÑÐ°Ð² Ð¿ÑÑ.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â OndÅej KolÃ¡Å (PPE). â Madam President, dear colleagues, later today we will be joined by the legitimate president of Belarus, together with her husband, a political prisoner, five years of whose life were stolen because he dared to oppose an autocrat. I was thinking of speaking about the necessity to protect democracy and human rights, freedom in all its forms, about the necessity to help those who do not enjoy living in a free country where the people decide on who will govern, not the governors. But let me offer you a different view.

We will not only be joined by the legitimate Belarusian president, but also by a wife and a mother, whose family was torn apart by a brutal and cynical thief of human lives. She was deprived of living her life with her beloved husband, their children were deprived of enjoying happy moments with their father. She was forced to leave her homeland, yet it did not break her. She chose to stand up strong and speak for thousands of people whose lives have been stolen. A brave woman who hasn't bowed to a tyrant, but chose to fight for her family and her country. And for that, she and her companions deserve our absolute support.

To conclude, let me ask all of us a question: are we doing enough? Do we have the courage to do even more to help a nation that is being suppressed? I hope we do. We have two great examples: a husband and wife, parents, two freedom fighters, Sviatlana Tsikhanouskaya and Siarhei Tsikhanouski.

(The speaker agreed to take a blue-card question)

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â OndÅej DostÃ¡l (NI), blue-card question. â Honourable colleague, I would like to ask one question. Belarus is not a member country of the European Union. What, in your opinion, is our right to judge who is leading the country? And what is our right to intervene into their domestic matters and to decide who is their legitimate president and who is not?

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â OndÅej KolÃ¡Å (PPE), blue-card answer. â I'm convinced it is our duty to stand up against those who deprive people of their rights, and that is what Alexander Lukashenka is doing. He is stealing the lives of Belarusians from them, and we, as a Union, who believe in human rights, who share certain values, must speak up for such people who are being suppressed by an autocratic regime.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Sabrina Repp (S&D). â Frau PrÃ¤sidentin, Herr Kommissar! Klatschen â das machen wir hier fast tÃ¤glich. Die einen lauter und fester, die anderen leiser und zÃ¶gerlicher. Kaum vorstellbar, dass Menschen aufgrund von Klatschen in der Ãffentlichkeit verhaftet werden. Doch genau das passiert immer wieder in Belarus. Dort werden Frauen oder auch junge Menschen bestraft, weil sie Gerechtigkeit fordern. Sie tragen Proteste. Wer frei denkt, riskiert Exmatrikulation, Haft und den Verlust der Zukunft.

Viele von ihnen sitzen heute in GefÃ¤ngnissen, andere leben im Exil. In Belarus reicht es, gemeinsam zu klatschen, um als GefÃ¤hrdung der Ã¶ffentlichen Ordnung zu gelten. Wir dÃ¼rfen nicht wegsehen. Politische Gefangene mÃ¼ssen unverzÃ¼glich freigelassen werden. Gleichzeitig braucht es gezielte Sanktionen gegen jene, die Folter und UnterdrÃ¼ckung anordnen, sowie konkrete UnterstÃ¼tzung fÃ¼r belarussische Studierende, Journalistinnen und Journalisten sowie Aktivistinnen und Aktivisten â sowohl vor Ort als auch im Exil â durch Stipendien, Visaerleichterungen und humanitÃ¤re Hilfe. Geben wir den Menschen in Belarus, die fÃ¼r ihre Freiheit klatschen, unseren Beifall â laut, klar und mit der Kraft aus der Mitte dieses Hauses. FÃ¼r Hoffnung auf eine europÃ¤ische Zukunft.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Stefano Cavedagna (ECR). â Signora Presidente, onorevoli colleghi, a cinque anni dalle elezioni del 2020, la Bielorussia rimane, purtroppo, ostaggio di un vero e proprio regime che calpesta qualsiasi principio democratico e di basilare libertÃ .

Lukashenko, al potere da oltre 30 anni, continua a reprimere il dissenso e, come abbiamo visto e parlato quest'oggi, si parla di oltre 1000 prigionieri politici detenuti in condizioni disumane e questo governo continua a portare avanti politiche che minano la lingua, la cultura, l'identitÃ  della nazione bielorussa.

Riconosciamo e riaffermiamo la solidarietÃ  dei conservatori al popolo bielorusso e in particolare a Sviatlana Tsichanouskaya, che Ã¨, a nostro avviso, una leader fondamentale della presenza democratica all'interno di questa nazione. Questa gestione del potere sta utilizzando la pressione dell'immigrazione clandestina di massa per destabilizzare la Polonia e l'est dell'Europa, similarmente a come qualcuno sta facendo nel sud dell'Europa e attraverso il Mediterraneo.

Difendiamo i nostri confini e la libertÃ  dei nostri popoli.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Dainius Å½alimas (Renew). â Madam President, dear colleagues, fully supporting the new resolution on the situation in Belarus, I want to highlight an uncomfortable truth. Since 2020, in numerous resolutions, this Parliament has called for justice and accountability. Yet what have we achieved? The EU Member States have not made any progress in prosecuting the perpetrators of crimes against humanity in Belarus, although we know their names.

So far, only Lithuania has invoked universal jurisdiction. Only Lithuania has referred the situation in Belarus to the International Criminal Court. And only Lithuania has applied to the International Court of Justice regarding the instrumentalisation of migrants by the Lukashenka regime.Â Yet even in Lithuania, no suspects have been named and no arrest warrants issued.

Needless to say, this approach is contrary to the concept of international crimes, in particular crimes against humanity. We should insist on concrete steps in improving the situation. Impunity makes the dictator laugh at us.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â MÄrtiÅÅ¡ StaÄ·is (Verts/ALE). â MadamÂ President, honourable Members, the people of Belarus do not stand with Lukashenka, and they don't stand with Putin either. And we share their dream of freedom and a country where the government serves its citizens, and does not imprison them, where the Belarusian language is celebrated, not suppressed, where allies treat Belarus with respect, not as a platform to start wars or for nuclear weapons.

We want to see Minsk stand proudly among the free capitals of the European Union, if that is the will of the people, and the day when Belarus competes in ice hockey under the white-red-white flag of free Belarus.

But Lukashenka stands against this, and Putin seeks to export this current Belarus model to Georgia and to as many countries as he can. Because the Kremlin fears freedom, because freedom proves that change is possible even in Russia. Long live free Belarus! Zhyve Belarus!

Â 
Â Â

Â Â Per Clausen (The Left). â Fru Formand! For fem Ã¥r siden blev valget i Belarus stjÃ¥let. Det skete igen i Ã¥r.

Jeg anerkender ingen af de to resultater, for valgene var pÃ¥ ingen mÃ¥de frie. Belarus er ikke frit, men derimod et brutalt diktatur. Et diktatur, der fÃ¦ngsler aktivister og journalister, demokratiforkÃ¦mpere og fagforeningsfolk.

For nylig satte Lukasjenko-regimet nogle politiske fanger fri, og det er en god dag, hver gang uskyldige fÃ¦ngslede slipper ud. Men vi mÃ¥ ikke vÃ¦re naive og tro, at alt nu er i orden, for det er det ikke. Vi har set det fÃ¸r i Belarus â hvordan styret sÃ¦tter nogle politiske fanger fri af hensyn til PR, men derefter fÃ¦ngsler endnu flere. Derfor skal vi heller ikke slÃ¦kke sanktionerne, de skal tvÃ¦rtimod gÃ¸re os effektive. For styret i Belarus er stadig illegitimt og undertrykkende, og undertrykkelse skal aldrig belÃ¸nnes.

Â Â Siegbert Frank Droese (ESN). â Frau PrÃ¤sidentin, verehrte Kollegen! Wir sprechen heute Ã¼ber WeiÃrussland und Ã¼ber Wahlbetrug. Was unterscheidet eigentlich die weiÃrussische Gesellschaft von â nun, sagen wirÂ â der Bundesrepublik Deutschland? In WeiÃrussland werden Oppositionelle bekÃ¤mpft, in der BRD werden Oppositionelle erst gar nicht zur Wahl zugelassen, wie es meinem AfDâKollegen Joachim Paul zur Wahl in Ludwigshafen geschehen ist. In WeiÃrussland lobt das Staatsfernsehen PrÃ¤sident Lukaschenko, in der BRD haben wir einen Staatsfunk, der die Regierung lobpreist und die Opposition systematisch ausgrenzt und diffamiert. In WeiÃrussland stehen Oppositionelle unter Druck, in der BRD dagegen wird die Opposition direkt vom Inlandsgeheimdienst verfolgt und observiert, mit dem Ziel, die Opposition ganz zu verbietenÂ â einmalig in Westeuropa. Also: Die Unterschiede sind hÃ¶chstens graduell, mit leichtem Vorteil fÃ¼r WeiÃrussland.

Meine Damen und Herren, Lukaschenko ist PrÃ¤sident, Zichanouskaja ist keine Heilige, und Deutschland gleicht immer mehr der DDR. Daher sollten wir erst einmal vor der eigenen TÃ¼r kehren, bevor wir uns Ã¼ber LÃ¤nder erheben und RatschlÃ¤ge erteilen.

Â Â Lukas Sieper (NI), Frage nach dem Verfahren der âblauen Karteâ. â Vielen Dank, Frau PrÃ¤sidentin! Im Einklang mit der GeschÃ¤ftsordnung wÃ¼rde ich auÃerdem gerne die MÃ¶glichkeit haben, auf die Antwort des Kollegen Droese nochmals zu reagieren. Herr Droese, Sie haben ja jetzt hier sehr ausfÃ¼hrlich darÃ¼ber gesprochen, warum das demokratische System in Deutschland, in Europa kaputt ist und wie sehr Ihre Partei gegÃ¤ngelt wird. Sie sparen dabei natÃ¼rlich wesentliche Punkte wie die offenen extremistischen Bestrebungen vieler Ihrer Kolleginnen und Kollegen aus.

Aber meine Frage ist dahingehend: Wann haben Sie das letzte Mal mitbekommen, dass ein Vertreter der Opposition â so wie Sie jetzt hier gerade â in WeiÃrussland im Parlament stand und die Regierung dermaÃen offen kritisiert hat? Und ist das nicht eigentlich der Unterschied zwischen Diktatur und Demokratie?

Â Â Siegbert Frank Droese (ESN), Antwort auf eine Frage nach dem Verfahren der âblauen Karteâ. â Herr Sieper, ich glaube, selbst Sie bekommen es mit, dass wir mittlerweile sehr gute InformationsmÃ¶glichkeiten haben, auch durch die Social Media, und da kann man alles nachlesen. Ich habe ja gerade die VerhÃ¤ltnisse dargestellt: Wir mÃ¼ssen uns eben in Deutschland â ich habe von Deutschland, nicht von anderen europÃ¤ischen LÃ¤ndern gesprochen â fragen lassen, wie weit wir die demokratischen Spielregeln sozusagen verinnerlicht haben, praktizieren, und ob wir nicht auf dem besten Weg sind, WeiÃrussland sozusagen in seiner Undemokratie zu folgen.

Â Â Lukas Sieper (NI), Reaktion nach dem Verfahren der "blauen Karte". â Wir sind deswegen, Herr Droese, auf dem besten Weg, WeiÃrussland nicht zu folgen, weil wir uns Populisten und Extremisten wie Ihnen und Ihrer Partei entgegenstellen. Ich denke, es spricht BÃ¤nde, dass Sie meine eigentliche Frage nicht beantwortet habenÂ â wie so oft, wenn man Sie und Ihresgleichen auf die Diffamierungen und die LÃ¼gen hinweist, die Sie in diesem Parlament verbreiten. Dann fÃ¤llt Ihnen nichts Besseres ein, als diese LÃ¼gen und diese Diffamierungen zu wiederholen. Diese Politik, diese Rhetorik, und wie Sie Angst und Unruhe in unserer BevÃ¶lkerung schÃ¼ren, das ist nichts weiter als Verrat an den europÃ¤ischen Idealen, Verrat an der europÃ¤ischen Idee, Verrat am deutschen Volk.

Â Â

Â Â OndÅej DostÃ¡l (NI). â MadamÂ President, dear colleagues, would you rather be a Belarusian or a Ukrainian? Belarus has cheap energies, oil and gas, their industry doesn't fail, their farmers don't strike, they export fertilisers around the world, they have a decent public health system and they are not short on critical medicines.

They are hindered only by sanctions, which harm us as well as them, and we should abolish them. But, more importantly, Belarus has peace. They still remember the horrors of a Nazi genocide much worse than in Poland. Their constitution explicitly excludes military aggression, and they are doing what they can to keep their country away from foreign wars.

Now, compare it to Ukraine after the Maidan putsch and after six years of Zelenskyy. The country destroyed, their sons dead, and those who return will find their land and minerals owned by foreign capital.

Belarusian leaders are not perfect. Which ones are? But if the West tries to impose on them some new Zelenskyy, they will look across their southern borders and say 'no, thank you'. We should respect that. Yes, it is not our country, but theirs.

Â 
Â Â 
Â

Â Â Thijs Reuten (S&D). â Madam President, colleagues, imagine not knowing anything about your loved ones for months, for years â convicted on trumped up charges and imprisoned under harsh conditions. It is the reality under the cruel regime of Putin's lapdog, Lukashenka.

It is again the reality for the family of opposition leader Mikalai Statkevich, released about six weeks ago â now again imprisoned and incommunicado. Maria Kalesnikava, Alex Bialiatski â incommunicado. It was the reality for years for a brave woman, Sviatlana, who carried a picture of her husband Siarhei everywhere. It's great to have them both here today.

For many, the horror of repression and terror continues. For Irina Tkachuk â illegally detained for defending her imprisoned daughter â her untreated health conditions risk irreparable harm. We demand an immediate end to the systematic repression of the Belarusian people and for the unconditional release of all political prisoners. Strengthen the sanctions and do not forget Belarus.

Zhyve Belarus!

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Reinhold Lopatka (PPE). â Madam President, five years ago, the people of Belarus stood up with extraordinary courage against the stolen election. They demanded democracy and were met with brutality.

Since then, Lukashenka and his regime have systematically destroyed the foundation of democracy, imprisoning more than 1Â 400 political activists, silencing independent media and turning repression into a tool of state control.

Let us be clear: this regime is not only repressing its own citizens. It is an active accomplice of Russia's war of aggression against Ukraine. And the recent release of Siarhei Tsikhanouski, after five years of unjust imprisonment, of course, is a rare moment of hope. It is proof that international pressure and European solidarity work are necessary. We have to continue with these efforts.

Â 
Â Â

Â Â Rasa JukneviÄienÄ (PPE). â Madam President, Sviatlana Tsikhanouskaya won the elections even though they were stolen from the Belarusian people through brutal force. Five years have passed, but no one in Belarus suddenly started loving the dictator Lukashenka.

The Belarusian opposition, together with the elected President, stands as an example for other nations fighting against the same Kremlin tyranny and its proxies. They are not only working tirelessly to raise awareness of their nation's suffering; they are also clearly shaping an alternative vision â a European, democratic Belarus.

Ukrainians, Moldovans, Georgians and Belarusians are all fighting for the same cause. They stand against Putin's aggressive Russian world â Russkiy Mir â ideology, which seeks to dominate a large part of Europe. Let's not grow tired of supporting Belarusian democratic forces. They are fighting for a safer, freer Europe. The more democracy there is to our east, the stronger and safer we all will be. Zhyve Belarus!

Â Â Mika Aaltola (PPE). â Madam President, dear colleagues, the situation in Belarus continues to be a barbaric attack on democracy and freedom. Five years ago, people voted for change and their will was stolen by Lukashenko's fraudulent regime.

Today, Belarus has become Moscow's vassal, complicit in Russia's war of aggression and even in the abduction of Ukrainian children to camps on its soil. These are crimes against humanity.

Europe must not forget. We must defend democracy through consistent pressure, real accountability and support for those who still believe in free Belarus.

Dictators must always know. Justice may be slow, but their doom will come. This is the only real deterrence and defence for democracy.

Â 
Â Â 
Â Â

Â Â Grzegorz Braun (NI). â MadamÂ President,Â get off, get back, leave Belarus as it is, because â good for them â they are not members of the European Union, of the eurokoÅchoz. I remember that Comrade Lenin once diagnosed that the Soviet Union cannot coexist with the outside world. Now, we have it like in a crooked mirror: the Euro Soviet cannot bear that other people live their lives.

So, let's stop harassing, let's stop warmongering, let's make business, let's have good contacts with all our neighbours. At least me, speaking for my Polish voters, I don't want to go to any war that you want to declare to anybody.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Vytenis Povilas Andriukaitis (S&D). â Madam President, dear colleagues, political prisoners in Belarus are subjected to torture, ill treatment and solitary confinement, like Maria Kalesnikava or Ales Bialiatski.

I would like to mention my old friend and social democratic opposition leader, Mikalai Statkevich. After being released, he refused to leave his country and his people, but he went missing. There is a high probability that he is allegedly imprisoned again.

Then there is the case of Anatol Kotau, a critic of the regime who disappeared in Turkey in August. We do not know where he is or what his current status and conditions are.

Political repression in Belarus must come to an end, and our role is now to support the opposition media in exile and civil society because they are the future of a European, democratic Belarus without Lukashenka.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Lukas Sieper (NI). â Madam President, dear people of Europe, imagine a country in the heart of our continent, rich in forests and fields, where people work hard to build, dream and create. Imagine a country that trades freely, that welcomes its neighbours not with fear, but with friendship. A country whose engineers, teachers and farmers shape the future not in silence but in pride. Imagine a Belarus that is not a prison, but a partner, a Belarus that stands with Europe, not against it.

That Belarus exists already in the hearts of millions of Belarusians who want nothing more than to live in freedom, dignity and peace. One day their courage will be stronger than their chains. And when that day comes, Europe must be ready to welcome Belarus home.

Â 
Â Â 
Â

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Dan JÃ¸rgensen, Member of the Commission. â MadamÂ President, thank you, honourable Members, for keeping Belarus on the international agenda and for your support to the Belarusian people on their democratic path.

We must see a fundamental shift from the Belarusian authorities to stop this brutal repression and adhere to international obligations. Without this, the selective release of political prisoners, while a welcome development, is a tactical gesture, not a systematic change. Even while these releases have taken place, repression against the Belarusian population has continued without interruption. If anything, it has intensified. Until we see such change, we will not relent in our approach. As outlined by PresidentÂ von der Leyen back in 2021, we have prepared a comprehensive EURÂ 3Â billion support plan for when a peaceful, democratic transition begins. This offer is on the table, ready once meaningful change occurs.

Five years on, the memory of the 2020 movement should be a source of inspiration for us all. We can all remember the extraordinary images of the streets of Minsk, filled with the ordinary Belarusians from all walks of life, peacefully claiming their right to an independent, democratic and sovereign future. This movement delivered a moment of great optimism about the future of Belarus, and captured the imagination of all of Europe. That same spirit drives our work today and we will continue striving for the goals it represents.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Die PrÃ¤sidentin. â Die Aussprache ist geschlossen.

Die Sitzung ist unterbrochen.

Sie wird in wenigen Minuten mit der Ansprache von Sjarhej Zichanouski und Swjatlana Zichanouskaja wieder aufgenommen.

EL peaks Valgevene opositsiooni toetama nii poliitiliselt, rahaga kui ka julgeoleku kÃ¼simustes. Samuti peaks EL Ã¤rgitama kolmandaid riike mitte tunnustama LukaÅ¡enkat Valgevene presidendina. Rahvusvahelistel foorumitel, nagu Euroopa NÃµukogu, OSCE ja NATO, tuleks Valgevene ametlike esindajatena kÃ¤sitleda riigi demokraatlikke jÃµude.

Valgevenes tuleb korraldada uued, vabad ja Ãµiglased valimised, mida hindaksid rahvusvahelised vaatlejad. Seejuures ei saa repressioonide Ãµhkkonnas toimuvad valimisi demokraatlikeks pidada. Â Â Sebastian Tynkkynen (ECR), kirjallinen. â Euroopan rajoilla vaanii VenÃ¤jÃ¤n lisÃ¤ksi myÃ¶s toinen julma vihollinen.Â KyseessÃ¤ on tietenkin Aljaksandr LukaÅ¡enkan hallitsema Valko-VenÃ¤jÃ¤.Â LukaÅ¡enka pieksee omaa kansaansa pysyÃ¤kseen vallassa.Â LukaÅ¡enka tyÃ¶nsi EU-rajoille vÃ¤lineellistettyjÃ¤ siirtolaisia Putinin kÃ¤skystÃ¤.Â LukaÅ¡enka ojensi Putinille auttavan kÃ¤tensÃ¤ ja tarjosi asemat venÃ¤lÃ¤isjoukoille, kun VenÃ¤jÃ¤ aloitti suurhyÃ¶kkÃ¤yksensÃ¤ Ukrainassa.Â Diktaattori LukaÅ¡enka, tÃ¤mÃ¤ on viestini teille:Â Euroopan silmissÃ¤ hallintonne ei ole yhtÃ¤Ã¤n Putinin VenÃ¤jÃ¤Ã¤ parempi.Â Valitsemanne tie on oleva tuhon tie.Â Se ei tapahdu ehkÃ¤ tÃ¤nÃ¤Ã¤n, mutta yhdestÃ¤ asiasta voitte olla varmoja.Â Putinin VenÃ¤jÃ¤ tulee luhistumaan ja teidÃ¤n oma hirmuhallintonne sen mukana.Â Joten luopukaa vallasta, ennen kuin on liian myÃ¶hÃ¤istÃ¤.

Â 
Â Â 
Â Â 

IN THE CHAIR: ROBERTA METSOLA
President

Â 

5. Resumption of the sitting
Video of the speeches
Â Â 

(The sitting resumed at 12.13)

Â 

6. Sakharov Prize 2025 (announcement of the winner)
Video of the speeches
Â 

Â Â President. â Good afternoon, dear colleagues, this afternoon. So following the decision that was taken at today's Conference of Presidents, it is my privilege to announce that the 2025 Sakharov Prize for Freedom of Thought has been awarded to Andrzej Poczobut from Belarus and Mzia Amaglobeli from Georgia.

Both are journalists currently in prison on trumped-up charges simply for doing their work and for speaking out against injustice. Their courage has made them symbols of the struggle for freedom and democracy. This House stands with them and with all those who continue to demand freedom.

In a few moments, we will hear an address from Sviatlana Tsikhanouskaya and Sergey Tihanovski. Their presence here today reminds us of what is at stake, and why this Parliament will never back down or look away from injustice.

I also want to acknowledge the other Sakharov finalists, who will be invited also in December here, namely journalists and aid workers in Palestine and students in Serbia.

I thank very much the political groups for their nominations and the Members of the Foreign Affairs Committee, Human Rights Subcommittee, and Development Committee for their work.

The award ceremony will take place here in Strasbourg, in this Chamber, during the December plenary session, and all the laureates will be invited.

Â 

7. Formal sitting - Address by Sviatlana Tsikhanouskaya and Siarhei Tsikhanouski
Video of the speeches
Â 

Â Â President. â Dear colleagues, today we are joined by our friends and global inspirations, Sergey Tihanovski and Sviatlana Tsikhanouskaya.

Dear Sergey, it is an honour and a joy to welcome you after five long years of brutal and unjust imprisonment by Lukashenkaâs regime. This House has been calling for your freedom since day one, and it is so good to see you finally free.

(Applause)

Dear Sviatlana, as a longstanding friend of this House and an outspoken voice both for Sergeyâs freedom and for the freedom of all Belarusians, it is an honour to welcome you to the Hemicycle once again.

(Applause)

Now, for the first time, you are with us together in Europeâs House of Democracy â reunited and ready to continue your shared fight for a free Belarus. Thank you both for agreeing to address us today, because yours is an incredible European story of endurance and the struggle for democracy, and this House applauds your courage and fortitude.

Just minutes ago, I announced Andrzej Poczobut from Belarus as a winner of this yearâs Sakharov Prize for Freedom of Thought, together with Mzia Amaglobeli from Georgia. Like you, dear Sergey, Andrzej was imprisoned under false charges and tragically is still jailed. And as members of Belarusâ democratic opposition awarded the 2020 Sakharov Prize, I am sure you both can appreciate this prizeâs importance in recognising Andrzejâs work and his plight.

Today, this House sends the clearest of messages to Andrzej Poczobut, to all those still cruelly and unfairly imprisoned by Lukashenkaâs regime, and to all Belarusians still hoping and pushing for a free, democratic future: the bonds that bind us Europeans cannot be broken. Europe understands that the age of dictators is over.

Over the last three years, Members of this House â many of you â have sent hundreds and hundreds of postcards carrying messages of hope and support to Belarusian political prisoners. I wrote one personally to Sergey. Last year, this House signed a letter of intent on cooperation with the Belarusian democratic forces which deepens our collaboration and condemns the acts of Lukashenkaâs regime. Now, dear friends and colleagues, we must build on this momentum and push for the release of the over 1Â 200 political prisoners still behind bars. As you say, dear Sviatlana, 'We canât stop, we have to keep working'.

Sergey, Sviatlana, your vocal defiance and your presence here today are proof that the fight for a free and democratic Belarus is alive and strong. Europe stands with you and will continue to support your efforts. Long live Europe, and long live free Belarus.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Sviatlana Tsikhanouskaya. â President Roberta Metsola, Vice-President, distinguished Members of the European Parliament, it is such a great honour to start the speech with such wonderful news. My sincere congratulations to Andrzej Poczobut, his family, and all Belarusians.

(Applause)

Of course, my congratulations go to Mzia Amaglobeli as well. I wish to thank the ECR and the EPP for nominating Andrzej Poczobut for the Sakharov Prize. I want to thank everyone who voted for his candidacy. Andrzej is a Belarusian Polish journalist who became a symbol of resistance and a personal enemy of the dictator. Before hearing his verdict of eight years in a high-security prison, he said, 'I am going to prison with a clear conscience. I always knew that when times like these come to Belarus, I will end up in prison.'

Awarding the Sakharov Prize to Andrzej and fearless Mzia Amaglobeli from Georgia sends a strong message to all political prisoners: 'You are not alone and journalism is not a crime'. As German parliamentarian Manuela Rothman once said: 'Today, heroes are not in the movies â heroes are in prisons'. And Andrzej and Mzia are among them. So, my congratulations. It is a really big victory.

Dear Roberta, thank you for your kind introduction. I donât know if you remember, but five years ago, in those days of our peaceful revolution, you were one of the first leaders I met. We had just won an election, and tens of thousands were already detained. You told me then: 'Donât give up. Keep fighting no matter what. We will always be here for you.'

And you kept your promise. From the very first day, the European Parliament stood on our side. While governments hesitated, you acted â denouncing Lukashenkaâs fake legitimacy, calling for sanctions, promoting visas and possibilities for Belarusians. Together, we signed a memorandum on cooperation between the European Parliament and the Belarus democratic forces, showing that Belarusâs rightful place is here, in the European Union. You took bold, unconventional steps that will be remembered in history books. Thank you.

And I want to thank each parliamentarian who adopted a political prisoner, signed a postcard, joined a solidarity rally, voted for resolutions and defended Belarus's right for self-determination. Thank you.

I want to thank the Delegation on Belarus, its amazing Chair, MaÅgorzata Gosiewska, and the standing rapporteur Helmut BrandstÃ¤tter for their leadership and bringing Belarus's voices here to Parliament. Also, I want to commend Lithuania and Poland, our closest neighbours and allies, who stand firm against Lukashenkaâs regime, support our movement, and continue to accept Belarusians fleeing terror.

And of course, big thanks to the European Commission, in particular PresidentÂ Ursula von der Leyen, Commissioner Marta Kos, and High Representative Kaja Kallas, for their steadfast support to Belarusian media, civil society, and human rights defenders, and for pledging the EURÂ 3Â billion plan, which we will implement after democratic change.

Change may not come in a single day, but I know it will come for sure. Today, we are building the rails of that change, so that one day we can place Belarus on the right track â the European track â that no Russia, and no Lukashenka, can ever block again. And yes, I believe that Belarus will be a success story, and we will all write this story together.

Dear friends, for five years, I was coming into this Chamber with a portrait of my husband. He was in Lukashenkaâs prison â isolated, starved, denied a voice. Three months ago, when I saw him for the first time, I could barely recognise him. He had lost sixty kilograms and looked like he returned from the gulag.Â But you know what? The next morning, he told me: 'Now, let's get to work.'

(Applause)

This is the essence of Belarus â beaten, exhausted, but never broken.

Siarhei brought new energy and strength to our movement. He is also catching up with a new reality. While he was behind bars, he missed our inspiring protests, he missed the migration crisis, brutal repression and the beginning of the war.Â He even missed his own Sakharov Prize ceremony.

Of course, Siarhei's release is my personal joy. But I cannot fully rejoice while thousands of families remain divided, while Ales Bialiatski cannot kiss his wife; while our brave Masha â Maria KalesnikavaÂ â cannot hug her father; while wives write letters to nowhere; while children fall asleep without their parents' lullaby.

Siarheiâs release was not a miracle. He is free thanks to you â your words, your pressure, your solidarity.Â It was you who, for all these years, kept fighting for Siarhei and the release of other political prisoners.Â His release proved that everything is possible if we are bold, consistent, and patient.

And it was a joint effort: a synergy of European pressure and American diplomacy. Here, I want to commend PresidentÂ Trump and his team â it was a remarkable humanitarian operation that saved 70 people.Â PresidentÂ Trump has now gone further. He has demanded from Lukashenka the release of all 1Â 300 political hostages â students, teachers, doctors, factory workers, journalists.Â We all pray to see more people free soon, including our common hero Andrzej Poczobut.

However, letâs not be fooled by Lukashenkaâs so-called 'humanity'. Letâs not be naÃ¯ve. He releases 60 and immediately arrests 120. Itâs like a revolving door.Â Even those 'pardoned' are not completely free: deported without documents, handcuffed, a sack over their head. Their families, still in Belarus, are harassed by the KGB.Â Those who refuse to leave, like Mikalai Statkevich, leader of the Belarusian Social Democrats, are thrown back into prison.

For Lukashenka, people are nothing but bargaining chips. He wants sanctions lifted, he wants legitimisation, he dreams of returning to 'business as usual'. But we have seen this movie before â the same show for 30 years. And we all know how it ends.

Repression continues not because the regime is strong, but because it is weak. Because people despise him.Â The dictator knows: the moment repression stops, Belarusians will rise again.Â And yes, dear parliamentarians, sanctions do work, no matter what the regime's apologists say. We see it already: sanctions force the regime to make concessions and to release people.Â The regime's economy is stagnating, the deficit keeps growing. He survives primarily on war contracts from Russia.Â Thatâs why now it is not the time to ease pressure â it is time to push harder and demand more.

Here I propose a two-track approach. While the US focuses on the humanitarian track â on releasing people and ending repression âÂ I call on the European Union to stay firm and principled.Â Let the US sanctions serve to save people's lives. But the EU sanctions must aim for systemic, irreversible change in Belarus.Â Keep the pressure until the regime stops jailing its own people, stops blackmailing Europe with migrants, stops arming Russia's war, and Belarus starts the path towards democracy. Your cards are stronger â and hold them until the right moment.

Donât be naÃ¯ve: Lukashenka will never change himself. But the country around him has already changed.Â The majority of Belarusians have chosen peace, democracy, and a European future. This future is impossible as long as Lukashenka remains in power.

Even those around him â nomenklatura â are whispering about transition. Everyone understands: change is inevitable. Many once believed the Berlin Wall would stand forever, just as some now believe the dictatorship in Belarus will never fall.Â But it will. The dictator's time is ticking. Lukashenka is becoming a lame duck.

This is what we must prepare for: a post-Lukashenka Belarus.Â We must make sure that not only Belarus is ready, but that Europe is ready when the moment comes.Â To do that, we must keep pro-European spirit alive inside the country: through more contacts, exchanges, and visas for ordinary Belarusians â open borders, cultural initiatives that strengthen national identity; through stronger support for independent media, such as our leading outlets Zerkalo, Belsat, Nasha Niva, or Radio Free Europe, reaching millions inside Belarus;Â through strengthening our democratic institutions, ready to assume responsibility when the window of opportunity opens.

We already developed a set of reforms â our own European roadmap â to ensure that Belarus moves towards democracy and to prevent Russia from taking over.Â But the first thing we need is free and fair elections. And let me remind you: five years ago, we already won elections by a landslide. And be sure, we will win it again.

(Applause)

But before all of that, one thing is paramount: Ukraine must win.

(Applause)

We admire President Zelenskyy and the bravery of the Ukrainian people. They are fighting not only for their own land; they fight for all of us. Ukraineâs success will open the path for all our nations living in the shadow of the empire â not only for Belarus, but also for Armenia, Georgia, and Moldova.

We Belarusians stand with Ukraine not only in words, but in deeds.Â Among our political prisoners, there are hundreds of heroes sentenced for solidarity with Ukraine, sabotaging Russian troops, and sharing information.Â As we speak, Belarusian volunteers from the KalinoÅ­ski Regiment are fighting shoulder to shoulder with Ukrainians on the frontlines.Â They asked me to pass on a message â the same one I heard from Roberta five years ago: 'Donât give up, Europe.'

When Ukraine is strong, Europe united and Russia weakened, change in Belarus will become only a matter of time. And this change can spark change in Russia too.

So, today I urge you: give Ukraine everything it needs, not just to sustain, but to win.Â I urge you: weaken Putin by sanctioning his oil revenues and confiscating his assets. The weaker Russia becomes, the weaker Lukashenka is.Â I urge you: bring perpetrators to justice â not only Putin, but also Lukashenka with his cronies. Support the Lithuanian referral to ICC for crimes against humanity. Never before we were so close to opening the case.

(Applause)

While keeping pressure on the regime, support our people â those inside the country and in exile. Thousands of Belarusians in the EU became de facto stateless, without documents, and face transnational repression.Â Invest in people, not dictators. Because military power alone means nothing without people ready to defend their values.

I urge you: see Belarus not only as a threat, but as an opportunity â to change the status quo and weaken Russiaâs presence on your eastern flank.Â Do not be afraid of dictators â let's make them afraid.

(Applause)

Dear friends, all the things I was saying today are already in your resolution to be voted on today. I deeply appreciate all the authors and groups who have contributed. It sends a clear message to the Belarusian people: 'You are not alone'. But also to the regime: 'We see your crimes'.Â It lays a solid foundation for a proactive EU strategy on Belarus, aimed to achieve solid and irreversible change. I will take this resolution to every government I meet, but above all, I will make sure that people inside the country see it and know that Europe stands with Belarus.

Dear Parliament, in conclusion, let me say a few words in Belarusian â first, because I want our language to be heard here in the European Parliament, and second, because I want to address my fellow Belarusians watching online.

Fellow Belarusians, those who are inside the country and those who fled Belarus. Never lose your hope. Never give up and be strong with your principles.

As Ales Bialiatski said: 'I believe because I know that the night passes and the day comes. And I know that what moves us forward is our hope and a dream'.

When we are together, when we believe, when we work for change, we will overcome. We will win.

Dear friends, now I want you to meet my personal hero, Siarhei Tsikhanouski, the man to whom I am grateful with all my heart for all he did and endured. After all, it was for him that I became an accidental politician.

Ladies and gentlemen, please welcome my husband, Siarhei Tsikhanouski.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â President. â Thank you, dear Sviatlana. Now, after five long years of imprisonment in which this House called consistently for your freedom, it gives us all a great pleasure to give the floor to Sergey Tihanovski.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Sergey Tihanovski. â Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, respected guests, I am not a professional diplomat, I am still learning. So please allow me to speak frankly, with little political correctness, just like General Kellogg did recently. This is what he said, commenting on his recent trips to Belarus, 'We were establishing the lines of communication with Putin, we weren't going in there initially to get political prisoners out.' It is a smart tactic on behalf of the American administration. I get it. Sometimes you need to talk to Mussolini before you talk to Hitler.

The stakes are high. Americans are ready to pay for the services of a middleman. The middleman gets what he wants, but he gives out hostages in return. At one point in these talks, a few months ago, Lukashenka promised to release one 'big name' prisoner â that was my big lucky moment. At the time, my name was the most well-known in the West, due to my wife's efforts. Lukashenka was trying hard to impress President Trump. That's why I got out.

I managed to survive five years of solitary confinement in the hell of the Belarusian gulag. Some of those I met there were not as lucky. We have been losing the best of our people. As for me, I know I would never have survived 20 years of my full sentence under those same conditions. The fate of Aleksei Navalny was very much in store for me.

There are still around 1Â 400 political prisoners in Belarus waiting for their turn to be free, and the number is growing by the day. We must get them all out. This issue has become my priority.

But what about the country of Belarus? Is it a lost cause?

During my time in prison, the dictator kept me completely isolated: no letters, no visits, no contact with the outside world. The only news I received was from state propaganda on the radio. It has been four months since my release, and I am quickly catching up with the world. But it is painful to see that things have gotten worse. The business of changing the regime in Belarus, that I started with other leaders five years ago, remains unfinished.

In the summer of 2020, the people of Belarus showed they've had enough. The number of people which took to the streets against Lukashenka was astounding. If you were to scale up this number relative to the population in Russia, it would be the equivalent of 15Â million people. No chance Putin would have survived such an open rebellion. For a short time, there was a moment we thought we could win. Some of Lukashenka's government officials were expressing sympathy, but stayed hesitant. They didn't flip to the side of the people. Why? Because when the time came, the Russians showed up for the fight â Europeans did not.

Because of Europe's hesitation, Belarus â a beautiful nation with strong Western, European values, this nation which was totally ready to become a member of the EU â was left alone and Moscow took over. This was not only tragic and unfair, it was also a serious strategic mistake. By failing to stop Putin in Belarus, Europe made the war in Ukraine almost inevitable. Hundreds of thousands killed, billions of dollars wasted, and we don't see the end of it.

Even if President Trump does the impossible and the fighting stops tomorrow, a hybrid war would continue, and trillions of euros will be spent on European defence. Just think how stupid it is â it could have cost a tiny fraction of this budget to change the game in Belarus in 2020. But that ship has sailed.

So what should be done now? How do we win this war? And what role does Belarus play in this?

In this complex, potentially very long hybrid war, victory will come only to those who take the initiative. My message is simple: Europe not only needs to become an active player, Europe needs to become an active player in Belarus. Lukashenka is a cunning survivor, but he is not immortal. The transfer of power in Belarus is imminent.

Here is the reality: since 2020, the people of Belarus have not accepted the dictator. They did not love him back. They did not revolt because they are simply being wise, careful and realistic. They understand that without any real allies, we are fighting a losing battle. Of course, Belarusians would love to join NATO, but after what happened in Ukraine, Belarusians will never believe we will ever be accepted. We are not that naÃ¯ve.

So how do we solve this problem? What's the formula? The answer can be found in Europe's own history. Think about Finland â think about Finland before it joined NATO. Finland learned how to live next to a difficult neighbour and still built a successful independent country without going to war. It was a careful balance, a smart strategy. Europe would benefit if Belarus became a neutral bridge between the EU and Russia. Belarusians would benefit as well. That would be a good thing for both sides, a definite win-win.

But, of course, Putin sees Belarus very differently. He sees it as a buffer zone, a military base he can use to pressure his neighbours, a country with limited independence, something like Chechnya, but in central Europe.

So how do we pull this off? There is only one solution: we need a third player in this political game. For many decades, the United States has saved you hundreds of billions of dollars each year, holding the security umbrella while Europeans were busy building a social welfare state. But today, America cannot be fighting on all three battlefields â in the Middle East, in Europe and in Asia â at the same time.

It is Europe, not the United States, that should take a leading role on its eastern borders. It is Europe that should become the main partner in helping Belarus move toward democracy. You are big, you are strong, your population is a whopping 500Â million, your GDP makes up 17Â % of the world total. Your total spending on helping Ukraine is a mere 0.1Â % â something tells me you can be doing a lot more. You just need confidence, you just need leadership, you just need to get serious.

We, the Belarusian democratic movement, have a plan. A plan for how to become the next Finland of eastern Europe. You have the tools, resources and the strength to make it real. It's your call now, Europe. Don't let us down â not like the last time.

Zhyve Belarus!

(The House rose and accorded the speaker a standing ovation)

Â 
Â Â 
Â Â 

(The sitting was briefly suspended)

Â 

8. Resumption of the sitting
Video of the speeches
Â Â 

(The sitting resumed at 12.49)

Â 

9. Penalties
Video of the speeches
Â 

Â Â President. â I have a couple of announcements and then I will give very, very short points of order.

We have almost two hours for the vote today.

Pursuant to Rules 10 and 183 of the Rules of Procedure and the Code of Appropriate Behaviour, and after taking into account the observations of the Member concerned, I have decided to impose a penalty on Zsuzsanna BorvendeÌg for her inappropriate behaviour, through offensive language and unethical actions towards Parliamentâs staff, which also undermined the dignity and reputation of Parliament.

This penalty consists of the forfeiture of her entitlement to the daily subsistence allowance for a period of 5 days.

The Member concerned has been notified of this decision. She lodged an internal appeal with the Bureau under Rule 184, but outside the applicable time period. So the Bureau, at its meeting of 20 October 2025, considered the internal appeal inadmissible and the penalty decision final, without prejudice to the external rights of appeal open to the Member concerned.

Â 

10. Request for waiver of immunity
Video of the speeches
Â 

Â Â President. â Second announcement: the competent authorities of Spain have addressed a request to me for the parliamentary immunity of Alvise PeÌrez to be waived.

The request is referred to the Committee on Legal Affairs.

Now, I have two points of order. I'll ask, once again, for the colleagues to be quick.

First, Paulo Cunha.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Paulo Cunha (PPE). â Senhora Presidente, caros colegas, o facto de hoje cada um dos 21 deputados portugueses estar aqui deveâse em boa parte a Francisco Pinto BalsemÃ£o. Ontem, soubemos da sua triste partida.

Fundador do Partido Social Democrata, antigo primeiroâministro de Portugal e atual conselheiro de Estado, Ã© uma personalidade incontornÃ¡vel da histÃ³ria portuguesa. Teve um papel fundamental na revisÃ£o constitucional de 1982, que abriu o caminho da adesÃ£o de Portugal Ã  entÃ£o denominada Comunidade EconÃ³mica Europeia.

A sua coragem, defesa intransigente das liberdades, da franqueza, do espÃ­rito empreendedor e de serviÃ§o ao paÃ­s sÃ£o caraterÃ­sticas pessoais e de carÃ¡ter que marcaram e marcarÃ£o, de forma determinante, o contexto passado, presente e futuro de Portugal.

Deixou sempre, em cada Ã©poca e circunstÃ¢ncia da vida nacional, a marca da sua audÃ¡cia e visÃ£o. Foi fundador de um jornal na ditadura e, mais tarde, da primeira televisÃ£o privada no nosso paÃ­s. Levou com ele um pouco de nÃ³s, mas deixouânos muito de si, a Portugal, mas tambÃ©m a toda a Europa.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â President. â Thank you very much, and sincere condolences.

Now Mr BrandstÃ¤tter.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Helmut BrandstÃ¤tter (Renew). â President Metsola, dear colleagues, I know there are some people in this House who support a war criminal and support Russia, but what one of our colleagues, Ms ÅoÅoacÄ, did recently in Moscow is even more. I quote the Kyiv Post, she said, 'If President Zelensky was to speak in my Parliament, I would break his legs'.

So it's an announcement of physical violence against the person, it's an announcement of physical violence against an elected president, and I think we all should know that. I am sure, President Metsola, you know what is the right thing to do.

Â Â

Â Â President. â Thank you very much. We will absolutely look into that.

11.1. Certain corporate sustainability reporting and due diligence requirements (A10-0197/2025 - JÃ¶rgen Warborn) (vote)
Video of the speeches
Â 

Â Â President. â The first vote is on the report by JÃ¶rgen Warborn, on behalf of the Committee on Legal Affairs, on certain corporate sustainability reporting and due diligence requirements (see minutes, item 11.1).

We have a round of speakers first. This is an important vote â it's good that we hear the colleagues.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â JÃ¶rgen Warborn, on behalf of the PPE Group. â MadamÂ President, I am speaking on behalf of a united EPP. We made an agreement with S&D and Renew, both on content, but also on the fact that we should have a committee mandate.

This committee mandate is important because we need to stick to the timetable â meaning that we go into trilogue already on Friday â so that we can deliver clarity for European businesses. Because, outside this Hemicycle, we have millions of businesses â small businesses, medium-sized ones, farmers, innovative scale-ups, large corporations â and they need clarity in order to deliver both sustainability and competitiveness, growth and good-paying jobs for Europeans.

So I urge colleagues, not least in S&D and Renew, to vote yes to the committee mandates.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Ana Catarina Mendes, em nome do Grupo S&D. â Senhora Presidente, caros colegas e caro colega que acaba de intervir, nÃ£o precisa de dar recados ao S&D, porque o S&D sabe bem que o compromisso alcanÃ§ado no quadro da plataforma democrÃ¡tica nÃ£o Ã© perfeito, mas garante o essencial: salvaguardas em matÃ©ria de direitos humanos, proteÃ§Ã£o ambiental e estabilidade para as nossas empresas.

Os Socialistas e Democratas estiveram na linha da frente destas negociaÃ§Ãµes, e lutÃ¡mos por uma lei mais justa, mais equilibrada e mais prÃ³xima das pessoas. E, mesmo perante as resistÃªncias e os bloqueios que nos colocou, agimos com responsabilidade, porque Ã© melhor o progresso, ainda que imperfeito, do que a paralisia.

Rejeitar este mandato seria um erro polÃ­tico grave, significaria abandonar a mesa das negociaÃ§Ãµes e abrir caminho Ã  incerteza, Ã  instabilidade e Ã  perda de credibilidade das instituiÃ§Ãµes europeias. Aprovar este texto Ã© garantir que a Europa continua a ser um espaÃ§o de confianÃ§a, de responsabilidade, de ambiÃ§Ã£o coletiva.

Mas deixo muito claro: os socialistas continuarÃ£o a lutar nos trÃ­logos por um resultado mais forte, mais justo e verdadeiramente europeu. E, por isso mesmo, aqui estaremos para construir e nÃ£o para criar bloqueios, como o PPE fez ao longo deste processo.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Pascale Piera, au nom du groupe PfE. â Madame la PrÃ©sidente, j'aimerais m'adresser plus particuliÃ¨rement Ã  mes collÃ¨gues du PPE. Le monde des entreprises vous regarde. Il vous reste encore une chance de rÃ©pondre Ã  leurs attentes lÃ©gitimes. Sur le devoir de vigilance et les obligations de reporting, certains d'entre vous ont cÃ©dÃ© au chantage des socialistes qui dÃ©fendent farouchement le pacte vert. Pourtant, vous le savez mieux que personne et vos Ã©lecteurs le savent mieux que personne, toutes ces normes tuent tous les jours nos industries, dÃ©truisent nos emplois sont la promesse du dÃ©clin Ã©conomique de notre continent.

J'en appelle Ã  votre responsabilitÃ©, Ã  celle de chacun d'entre vous. Ne sacrifiez pas l'avenir de vos entreprises contre de petits calculs politiciens. La situation Ã©conomique est trop grave pour se dÃ©rober. Je vous propose qu'ensemble, nous puissions mettre un terme Ã  cette ivresse normative pour enfin renouer avec la vraie prospÃ©ritÃ©. Je vous propose qu'ensemble, nous votions contre l'envoi de ce texte en trilogue pour pouvoir, Ã  la prochaine plÃ©niÃ¨re, avoir une majoritÃ© qui soit connectÃ©e au rÃ©el, une majoritÃ© qui entende enfin la dÃ©tresse de nos entreprises.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Tobiasz BocheÅski, on behalf of the ECR Group. â Madam President, honourable Members, today all of us have a chance to make an important decision. Do we want to strengthen the competitiveness of the European economy, or do we prefer to remain on the all-bureaucratic path? Omnibus 1 needs to be amended and we are proposing to continue working on it. We must demonstrate our respect for the democratic process and our commitment to a pro-business approach.

Therefore, I urge you to vote against the Committee on Legal Affairs motion for interinstitutional negotiations. Let us continue our work on this matter in a plenary here.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Pascal Canfin, au nom du groupe Renew. â Madame la PrÃ©sidente, chers collÃ¨gues, un compromis, c'est un compromis. Ãvidemment, il est toujours trop Ã  gauche pour certains, toujours trop Ã  droite pour d'autres. Mais un compromis, c'est mieux que l'esprit d'extrÃ©misme et l'esprit de polarisation. Et donc nous, au sein du groupe Renew, nous valoriserons et nous soutenons ce compromis, aussi imparfait soit-il, comme tout compromis. Car il est hors de question de laisser la main aux extrÃªmes de ces hÃ©micycles. Il est hors de question de laisser l'esprit de polarisation, de clivage, de division des antieuropÃ©ens diviser l'Europe et notre capacitÃ© de compromis entre les forces proeuropÃ©enne. Donc nous serons tous unis, tous unis au sein du groupe Renew pour soutenir ce compromis.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Kira Marie Peter-Hansen, on behalf of the Verts/ALE Group. â MadamÂ President,Â colleagues, let's be honest: this is not simplification, this is deregulation and a complete dismantling of European sustainability and due diligence rules.

Some have tried to paint the picture that certain negotiators have not been willing to compromise. But this picture is far from reality. I have experienced colleagues who were competent and ready to compromise on everything: on the scope, on climate transition plans and on civil liability. But the compromise that lies at hand does not reflect a compromise; it reflects political blackmail. And this mandate removes civil liability, it weakens climate transition plans and it empties out the scope drastically.

If the chaos in the last couple of weeks has shown us anything, it is that if you want a stable, pro-European majority, having the Greens on board is probably a good idea.

Colleagues, we can and we must do better than this. So give us a chance to make this law meaningful, to compromise and to ensure that it has a shred of civil liability included.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Manon Aubry, au nom du groupe The Left. â Madame la PrÃ©sidente, chers collÃ¨gues, les lobbies l'ont rÃªvÃ©, vous l'avez fait. Avec ce texte omnibus, vous signez l'arrÃªt de mort du devoir de vigilance qui mettait un terme Ã  l'impunitÃ© des multinationales en les rendant juridiquement responsables de leurs violations des droits humains et dommages Ã  l'environnement. Avec ce texte, vous cÃ©dez une fois de plus aux injonctions de Donald Trump, qui a explicitement exigÃ© sa suppression. Avec ce texte, vous donnez un totem d'immunitÃ© aux entreprises comme Total ou Shein pour continuer Ã  exploiter la nature et les travailleurs.

Et en plus, Ã  la demande de l'extrÃªme droite, ce vote aura lieu Ã  bulletin secret, ce qui est assez inÃ©dit. On voit qu'ils ont la transparence Ã  gÃ©omÃ©trie variable et quand vous dÃ©truisez nos droits, vous prÃ©fÃ©rez le faire bien tranquillement, dans l'ombre. Alors je voudrais m'adresser aux dÃ©putÃ©s socialistes qui ont contribuÃ© Ã  sceller ce pacte et qui s'apprÃªtent Ã  voter ce deal de la honte. Qu'allez-vous dire aux victimes des crimes des multinationales qui espÃ©raient obtenir justice grÃ¢ce Ã  ce texte...

(La PrÃ©sidente retire la parole Ã  l'oratrice)

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Mary Khan, im Namen der ESN-Fraktion. â Frau PrÃ¤sidentin! Mit dem sogenannten Green Deal hat Ursula von der Leyen den Niedergang unserer europÃ¤ischen Wirtschaft beschlossen, und die Omnibus-Verordnung hÃ¤tte die Chance geboten, wenigstens einen Teil des Schadens zu begrenzen. Doch statt unsere Unternehmen zu entlasten, haben sich die europÃ¤ischen Christdemokraten wie so oft fÃ¼r die Brandmauer entschieden statt fÃ¼r unsere Industrie. Jetzt haben Sie erneut die MÃ¶glichkeit, etwas gut zu machen. Befreien Sie Tausende, Tausende Unternehmen von der grÃ¼nen Lieferketten-BÃ¼rokratieÂ â damit sie schlicht Ã¼berleben kÃ¶nnen. Stimmen Sie heute gegen das Verhandlungsmandat, damit neue AntrÃ¤ge eingebracht werden kÃ¶nnen, um echte Entlastung zu schaffen.

Und vor allem an die CDU gerichtet: Sie haben die Chance, noch mehr Unternehmen zu entlasten. Und das tun Sie nicht fÃ¼r eine Mehrheit hier. Das tun Sie fÃ¼r die BÃ¼rger, die Sie gewÃ¤hlt haben, und vor allem fÃ¼r die Zukunft unseres Landes.

Â Â President. â Please, everybody, put the cards in the machines. Check that they're working.

I'd like to remind you â this is important â that if you support the committee's decision to enter negotiations, you should vote in favour. If you do not support it, you should vote against.

So we will vote on the decision to enter interinstitutional negotiations. It is by secret ballot and the vote is open.

11.2. Draft general budget of the European Union for the financial year 2026 â all sections (vote)

â After the vote:

Â Â Marie Bjerre, President-in-Office of the Council. â Madam President, honourable Members, Commissioner, the European Parliament has just adopted amendments to the Council's positions on the draft budget for the financial year 2026.

I take note of the differences in the positions of our two institutions concerning the draft budget 2026 presented by the Commission. Consequently, in my capacity of President of the Council, I agree that the President of the European Parliament convenes the Conciliation Committee as required in Article 314(4)(c) of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union.
