14. The ongoing assault on the democratic institutions and the rule of law in Bulgaria (topical debate)
Video of the speeches
Â 

Â Â Presidente. â L'ordine del giorno reca la discussione di attualitÃ  richiesta da un gruppo politico, Renew (articolo 169 del regolamento), sul tema "I continui attacchi alle istituzioni democratiche e allo Stato di diritto in Bulgaria".

Ricordo agli onorevoli deputati che per questa discussione non Ã¨ prevista la procedura del catch-the-eye nÃ© saranno accettati interventi "cartellino blu".

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â ValÃ©rie Hayer, author. â MadamÂ President,Â dear colleagues, dear Commissioner, dear Minister, a democratically elected mayor is jailed for no reason. Blagomir Kotsev, along with others, becomes a political prisoner inside the European Union.

This is a warning for all of us. The Mayor of Varna was elected by the Bulgarian people to bring reform, transparency and hope. Today, he sits in a high-security cell without credible charges, without fair process, without daylight. He is jailed for what he represents: a new generation that refuses corruption and Kremlin-style power.

This is not about a single politician. This is a European story, and it is a disgrace for us as a free and democratic European Union.

We won't turn a blind eye on Sofia. This is a test. This is a test of our values, of our credibility and of our courage. Because if justice can be twisted for one, it can be twisted for all. And when we stay silent, we become partners in crime.

Colleagues, Europe cannot be strong abroad if it is blind at home. We cannot preach democracy in Kyiv and Tbilisi while ignoring injustice in Sofia. This is the moment to show that Europe still stands for democracy and justice â everywhere and for everyone.

Let's not fool ourselves. What we witness in Bulgaria happens not by accident. It happens in a country where justice has been captured by oligarchs and where those who stand for reform are treated as enemies of the state. This is the corrupted world of Delyan Peevski and his ally Boyko Borisov. Their despotic system silenced judges, prosecutors, security services, media. Delyan Peevski is a danger for democracy. One by one, he is taking control of the existing political parties. One by one, those political parties are used as tools to silence opponents, while in the past they used to struggle for democracy in the country.

Let me be clear: the dimension is huge. Bulgaria is part of NATO's Black Sea strategy â a cornerstone of our collective defence. When corruption and political persecution take root there, it weakens Europe's security and gives power to those who wish us to decline.

We urge the European Commission to send an urgent fact-finding mission to Bulgaria to assert the rule of law and the detention of opposition leaders. To enforce conditionality, we call for the suspension of all Recovery and Resilience Facility payments linked to judicial reform until political prisoners are freed and until Bulgaria demonstrates true separation of powers.

Dear colleagues, it is our duty â as liberal democrats, as Christian democrats, as social democrats â to intervene. Your parties in the EPP, in the Socialists, might be part of the current government, but your honour and values must prevail over party politics. So move on. Use your European families to apply the necessary pressure. Our duty is to act with clarity, unity and courage. We call for change in Bulgaria now.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Marie Bjerre, President-in-Office of the Council. â Madam President, honourable Members, Commissioner, the rule of law and democracy are core values of the European Union. The two are closely linked. The rule of law is the fuel on which democracy runs. It ensures that the law applies equally to all. It ensures that judges can rule without fear or favour. It guarantees a stable legal framework. It provides a system of checks and balances that also protects the rights of minorities.

For the Council, the rule of law is a constant priority. This is why we have a regular rule of law dialogue, where we examine the specific situation in each Member State.

In this context, Bulgaria will feature in the next discussions and at the General Affairs Council on 17 November, together with three other Member States. There we will look at recent developments in Bulgaria and address challenges and recommendations as identified by the Commission in its annual Rule of Law Report.

These rule of law dialogues facilitate constructive discussions. They help us stay ahead of potential issues and help Member States address rule of law issues. Our democratic institutions face threats from both internal and external sources, polarisation, foreign interference. disinformation, manipulation of elections, to quote just the most concerning ones.

We must find ways to address those challenges together. I am also confident that the upcoming European Democracy Shield will offer us additional tools.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Dan JÃ¸rgensen, Member of the Commission. â MadamÂ President, honourable Members, thank you for the opportunity to exchange with you about the rule of law in Bulgaria.

The Commission shares the objectives of the Parliament as regards the promotion and protection of the rule of law. It is essential that all the EU institutions work in close cooperation and coordination to this end. The rule of law report stands as a crucial instrument in advancing our shared objectives.Â With the sixth edition of this report, we once again see that it has a real impact across the EU. The report serves as a decisive tool that actively shapes and influences the reforms being implemented in many Member States. Furthermore, the report has proven itself as the foundation for a genuine political dialogue in the rule of law at both European and national level.

First, allow me to delve into the findings and recommendations of the 2025 Rule of Law Report and its chapter dedicated to Bulgaria. The 2025 report notes that Bulgaria has engaged in reform efforts in dialogue with the Commission under the rule of law cycle and the recovery and resilience plan, and there have been several legislative developments, particularly since the beginning of the year.

Starting with the justice system, there has been several such positive developments. A draft law to extend judicial review for prosecutorial decisions, for cases of determination and suspension of investigations for victimless crimes, was tabled. Moreover, the law of a mandatory information meeting on mediation has been adopted, and new digital tools have been introduced to improve access to justice.

However, following the Constitutional Court decision of July 2024, some provisions from the December 2023 comprehensive constitutional reform have been declared unconstitutional, namely the provisions considering unconstitutional and which have been dismissed, including limits on the Prosecutor-General's powers, the mechanism for the effective accountability and criminal liability of the Prosecutor-General and deputies was declared constitutional. Yet, it should be noted that the effectiveness of this mechanism is constrained by some procedural issues.

Furthermore, regarding the Supreme Judicial Council, the Constitutional Court dismissed the overall reform, but considered that a restructuring would be possible. As regards other aspects of the justice system, the long-term secondment of judges and the functioning of the inspectorate through the Supreme Judicial Council remains a concern. As regards the anti-corruption framework, on the positive side, legislation on lobbying transparency is under preparation, and further amendments to the legislation on the protection of whistle-blowers has been entered into force.

More efforts are needed, however, as regards other measures. The report notes that the implementation of the national anti-corruption strategy remains limited. A robust track record of high-level corruption cases is yet to be established. Important gaps remain also in the rules of the integrity of persons with top executive functions. Some steps have been taken to implement the reform of the Commission for Counteracting Corruption and Illegal Assets Forfeiture, but further steps are needed to ensure the effective performance of the Anti-Corruption Commission, in particular to guarantee its independence.

In the context of the Recovery and Resilience Facility, the European Commission found that the milestone related to this reform has not yet been satisfactorily fulfilled. As a result, the Commission proposed a temporary deferred portion of the payment.

As regards media pluralism and media freedom and Bulgaria, concerns regarding the funding and politicisation of the media regulator persists. Furthermore, the draft law aimed at strengthening the independence of the public sector media was not yet adopted, and the appointment of a new director-general of the Bulgarian national television continues to be delayed. While access to public information is improving, it is still hindered by previously existing obstacles, with journalists facing increasing challenges and activities. On the positive side, improvements have been achieved regarding the transparency and allocation of state advertising.

The EU also continues to ensure adequate follow-up by its Member States to the directive, adopted in 2024, following on from the recommendation, to protect journalists and civil society from abusive lawsuits, and they act as a public watchdog. We are supporting Member States to ensure the transposition of the directive by the deadline of 7Â May next year. We invite Bulgaria to be ambitious in their transposition and cover also domestic cases in order to enable their courts to adequately handle all SLAPPs and provide safeguards to those targeted with SLAPPs.

The practical implementation of rule of law-making continues to face challenges, and concerns remain regarding the quality of the legislative process. Some independent and regulatory authorities continue to operate with an expired mandate and the renewal processes have been relaunched. On the positive side, attempts to reintroduce legislation required the registration of organisations receiving foreign funding were again dismissed by Parliament.

We follow up on all of these developments and the implementation of the report's recommendations with Bulgaria through close engagements, as we do with all Member States. I look forward to hearing your views.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Lena DÃ¼pont, on behalf of the PPE Group. â MadamÂ President, Commissioner, the EU is based on the principles of the rule of law. We promote it for good reasons, as a basic political toolbox that allows societies to thrive and businesses to grow. As such, it requires constant work and good care. It binds all political actors, whether in opposition or in government.

At the heart of today's debate is the question of proper checks and balances, and, more specifically, of the independence of the judiciary. So, in principle, today's debate shows the importance of the rule of law for all of us. But why do I say 'in principle'? Because exactly that independence has been put into question by some communications.

So let's look into the example at hand: the case of the Mayor of Varna. For that case, the anti-corruption framework and structure is relevant â which, by the way, was put in place by the previous government. The allegations are about being part of an organised criminal group and about significant bribes in connection to public procurement â for sure something for the Commission for Anti-Corruption.

Detention has been upheld by five different courts. Although not related to the case, GERB has signalled support for the release from detention. But an independent institution is doing what an independent institution should do: it's investigating, free from political influence.Â Having said that, does that mean that there is no room for improvement? I think we should always aim for better.

The 2025 Rule of Law Report describes the task, but for the first time after four elections in a row, we now have a stable government. So, colleagues, let's be really careful with the difference between improving and reforms, and, I quote here, 'the ongoing assaults on institutions'.

Â 
Â Â 
Â

Â Â Julien Leonardelli, au nom du groupe PfE. â Madame la PrÃ©sidente, chers collÃ¨gues, il y a quelque chose de profondÃ©ment ironique Ã  voir aujourd'hui le groupe Renew, fondÃ© par Emmanuel Macron, prÃ©tendre donner des leÃ§ons d'Ã©tat de droit et de dÃ©mocratie Ã  la Bulgarie. Car enfin, qui, en France, concentre le pouvoir entre quelques mains? Qui contourne la volontÃ© populaire? Qui gouverne dans le mÃ©pris du peuple? Eh bien c'est le mÃªme Emmanuel Macron. Et voilÃ  que ses reprÃ©sentants, ici Ã  Strasbourg, s'Ã©rigent en gardiens de la morale dÃ©mocratique, tout en soutenant un systÃ¨me europÃ©en qui piÃ©tine les votes des nations quand ils ne vont pas dans le bon sens.

Nous, les patriotes pour l'Europe, avec Jordan Bardella, nous affirmons que la vÃ©ritable dÃ©mocratie ne se dÃ©crÃ¨te pas depuis les couloirs de la Commission europÃ©enne. Elle s'enracine dans la souverainetÃ© des peuples et dans le respect de leurs choix. L'Europe ne se sauvera pas contre les nations, mais avec elles. VoilÃ  le sens de notre combat: dÃ©fendre une Europe des peuples libres, fidÃ¨le Ã  ses racines, Ã  ses voix et Ã  la dignitÃ© dÃ©mocratique de chacun de ses Ãtats.

Â Â

Â Â Nikola Minchev, on behalf of the Renew Group. â Madam President, Mr Commissioner, dear colleagues, every year the Commission's rule of law report uses the same phrase: lack of progress. But the truth is that the reports are much softer than reality is on the ground.

Let's be clear, pre-trial detention instead of being a measure of last resort is used as a measure by default. A democratically elected mayor is given an ultimatum to choose between switching party allegiance and a new beginning for his mandate, or be arrested. He chose integrity and he is still in custody.

We have an anti-corruption commission, the work programme of which involves only representatives of the opposition, even violating the independence of the National Bank simply to remove from its board an opposition nominee.

We have an acting prosecutor general who clings to his office long after his mandate has expired. Even after the Supreme Court confirmed that he does not have the legal authority to serve. We have an electoral process with countless violations, summed up best by the sincere, 'who gives the money now?', said by a voter immediately after leaving the voting room?

And don't get me started on media freedom and pluralism. It's time for Europe to face these issues head on. The President of the Commission must deliver on her promise that respecting the rule of law should be bound to the access of EU funds.

European parties, too, must hold their own members to the same standards they preach. We cannot afford to repeat the same mistake we made with OrbÃ¡n. Nothing fuels the anti-European narrative more than Europe closing its eyes in front of corruption and wrongdoing.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Daniel Freund, on behalf of the Verts/ALE Group. â MadamÂ President, dear colleagues, when the key witness in a case admits that he has been coerced to testify â and not by some criminal gang, but by the authorities â then you know that something is severely wrong. And this is a case in Bulgaria.

The country had been making progress in the last few years: joining Schengen this January, the euro area in January next year. But now we see that some of the hard-won reforms, particularly for democracy and the rule of law, are being dismantled by the current government.

Blagomir Kotsev, the Mayor of Varna, was arrested in July. To this day, the charges against him remain legally unsubstantiated. What the authorities seem to be holding against him is that he is a pro-European opposition politician in Bulgaria, and that he is a member of a party that wants to fight corruption.Â So, when the coerced witness withdrew his statement, that should have been the end of the case, but apparently it is still not â it keeps on going. How is this possible?

The Commission needs to seriously investigate what's going on with the rule of law, and we need to look into whether the conditions for paying EU funds are still fulfilled.

Â

Â Â ChloÃ© Ridel (S&D). â Madame la PrÃ©sidente, chers collÃ¨gues, en Bulgarie, la machine de dÃ©stabilisation russe tourne Ã  plein et menace de faire que ce pays, aprÃ¨s quelques annÃ©es Ã  construire sa dÃ©mocratie, ne sombre dans l'illibÃ©ralisme. Il y a des signaux inquiÃ©tants, comme l'arrestation du maire de Varna, un opposant. Il y a aussi des lois discriminantes envers la communautÃ© LGBTQIA+. Cette dÃ©rive doit Ãªtre stoppÃ©e maintenant pour que la Bulgarie ne passe pas d'un modÃ¨le tel que celui de l'Europe dÃ©mocratique Ã  l'illibÃ©ralisme. Parce que cette dÃ©rive menace tout ce qui fonde notre union, l'Ã©tat de droit, la dignitÃ© humaine et la libertÃ©.

Aussi, l'Europe ne peut pas dÃ©tourner le regard. Et nous devons veiller, la Commission doit veiller, Ã  ce que les fonds europÃ©ens destinÃ©s Ã  la Hongrie ne nourrissent pas la rÃ©pression politique dans ce pays, ou la corruption. Nous demandons Ã  ce que les fonds europÃ©ens soient examinÃ©s tant que l'indÃ©pendance de la justice n'est pas complÃ¨tement garantie. DÃ©fendre la dÃ©mocratie bulgare, c'est dÃ©fendre l'Europe.

Â Â Carolina Morace (The Left). â Signora Presidente, onorevoli colleghi, la Bulgaria riceve ogni anno 600 milioni di euro dall'Unione europea e, anche grazie a questi fondi europei, dal 2007 â anno in cui Ã¨ entrata nella nostra casa europea â il suo PIL Ã¨ cresciuto del 160%. La Bulgaria poi, dal prossimo 1Âº gennaio entrerÃ  ufficialmente nell'Eurozona e i suoi cittadini potranno utilizzare la moneta unica.

A fronte di questi risultati positivi, la Bulgaria non ha fatto nessun passo avanti nel campo dei diritti civili e sociali. Se io mi fossi sposata in Italia con una cittadina bulgara anzichÃ© con un'australiana, quel matrimonio sarebbe stato carta straccia nel suo Paese. La Corte europea dei diritti dell'uomo ha condannato la Bulgaria per essersi rifiutata di riconoscere l'unione di due donne contratta all'estero. Ma niente: la Bulgaria resta nel Medioevo.

Se questa Unione europea non fosse solo un bancomat, ma una comunitÃ  di cittadini che riconosce diritti e valori, allora userebbe ogni strumento legale e diplomatico per cambiare le cose.

Â Â AdriÃ¡n VÃ¡zquez LÃ¡zara (PPE). â SeÃ±ora presidenta, en los Ãºltimos cinco aÃ±os Bulgaria ha celebrado cinco elecciones legislativas. Estos cinco procesos electorales son el resultado de la incapacidad en la que se ha asentado el paÃ­s para formar gobiernos estables, abocando a Bulgaria a una eterna espiral de convocatorias anticipadas.

Sin embargo, la peor noticia de toda inestabilidad polÃ­tica es cÃ³mo ha sido aprovechada y potenciada por movimientos polÃ­ticos prorrusos para ganar influencia dentro de la UniÃ³n Europea. No podemos olvidar que una gran mayorÃ­a de los problemas internos e institucionales que sufre Bulgaria estÃ¡n directamente ligados a estas injerencias.

Y sÃ­, Bulgaria necesita seguir avanzando en reformas para profundizar en el Estado de Derecho y en la independencia judicial âel informe de la ComisiÃ³n Europea es muy claroâ. Pero yo creo que Bulgaria, sobre todo, necesita uniÃ³n. UniÃ³n entre las fuerzas proeuropeas para contrarrestar los ataques de Putin.

Yo creo que es el momento de apartar cuitas internas entre las fuerzas democrÃ¡ticas bÃºlgaras y apoyar al Gobierno en su postura clara en defensa de la integraciÃ³n europea y su visiÃ³n prooccidental. Y, por supuesto, continuar construyendo un Estado democrÃ¡tico pleno.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Juan Fernando LÃ³pez Aguilar (S&D). â Madam President, Commissioner, we have put in place a rule of law, fundamental rights and democracy framework, which encompasses all of the 27 Member States in the individual country reports. And six years in a row we have learned lessons.

The first lesson: we have to tell the difference as to those cases in which there is a serious, clear risk of a serious breach and violations of the values enshrined in Article 2, and then leading to sanctions, according to Article 7 â that was the case of Hungary, it was the case of Poland right after, but then Poland was brought out from this sanction procedure, but Hungary stays â and those cases in which there are matters to be worried about, to keep an eye on, which do not indicate a clear risk of a serious breach.

That's the case of Bulgaria.Â Yes, we know that Mayor Kotsev is under arrest and pre-trial investigation. We have to let the judicial procedure carry its own way. But having said this, we see a situation of political instability, that has been mentioned, that prevents the supreme court to be renewed. The prosecutor general has just expired, and the mandate is also to be renewed. And more than that, we have to tackle foreign interference, Russian interference, making an impact, a negative impact on a country that has won its access, not only to the euro, but also ...

(The President cut off the speaker)

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Sophie WilmÃ¨s (Renew). â Madame la PrÃ©sidente, l'indÃ©pendance de la justice, ce n'est pas un dÃ©tail technique et encore moins une vue de l'esprit, c'est le cÅur mÃªme de l'Ã©tat de droit. Et je rejoins mes collÃ¨gues, BlagomirÂ KotsÃ©v a droit aussi Ã  la justice et Ã  l'Ã©galitÃ© devant la loi.

Dans son rapport de 2025, la Commission alertait dÃ©jÃ  sur l'Ã©tat de l'Ã©tat de droit en Bulgarie, et avec la constatation que les inquiÃ©tudes autour de cette situation s'accumulentÂ â on l'a aussi d'ailleurs entendu Ã  cette tribuneÂ â, et c'est pourquoi, en tant que prÃ©sidente du groupe de suivi sur la dÃ©mocratie, lâÃ©tat de droit et les droits fondamentaux (DRFMG), j'ai proposÃ© que nous nous rendions sur place. Je pense que c'est notre travail, je pense que c'est notre rÃ´le et je dois vous dire que je suis assez surprise qu'Ã  ce stade il ne soit pas possible, en tout cas il semble impossible Ã  ce stade, d'avoir une majoritÃ© pour conduire une mission du DRFMG en Bulgarie. C'est assez Ã©tonnant. J'entends qu'on la soutientÂ â mais oui, on la soutientÂ â, l'annÃ©e prochaine peut-Ãªtre. Ce qu'il faut faire, c'est partir maintenant.

Ã celles et ceux qui nous expliquent qu'il n'y a rien Ã  voir, eh bien je leur dis Â«N'ayez crainte, alorsÂ», le DRFMG va faire une mission qu'on appelle mission d'information et il reviendra pour apaiser tout le monde.

J'en appelle donc Ã  celles et ceux qui doivent encore se dÃ©cider pour cette mission, parce qu'il est important de transcender nos diffÃ©rences politiques quand il s'agit de dÃ©fendre l'Ã©tat de droit.

ÐÐ¾Ð³Ð°ÑÐ¾ ÐµÐ´Ð½Ð° Ð´ÑÑÐ¶Ð°Ð²Ð° ÑÐµ Ð´Ð²Ð¸Ð¶Ð¸ Ð½Ð°Ð¿ÑÐµÐ´, Ð½Ð°Ð¹âÑÑÐ¼Ð½Ð¸ ÑÐ° Ð¾Ð½ÐµÐ·Ð¸, ÐºÐ¾Ð¸ÑÐ¾ ÑÐ° ÑÐ²Ð¸ÐºÐ½Ð°Ð»Ð¸ Ð´Ð° Ñ Ð²Ð¸Ð¶Ð´Ð°Ñ Ð² Ð·Ð°ÑÑÐ¾Ð¹. ÐÐ¾ ÑÑÐ¼ÑÑ Ð½Ðµ Ð¿ÑÐ¾Ð¼ÐµÐ½Ñ ÑÐ°ÐºÑÐ¸ÑÐµ. ÐÑÐ»Ð³Ð°ÑÐ¸Ñ Ðµ ÑÑÐ°Ð±Ð¸Ð»Ð½Ð°, Ð´ÐµÐ¼Ð¾ÐºÑÐ°ÑÐ¸ÑÐ½Ð° Ð¸ Ð°Ð½Ð³Ð°Ð¶Ð¸ÑÐ°Ð½Ð° Ñ Ð²ÑÑÑÐ¾Ð²ÐµÐ½ÑÑÐ²Ð¾ÑÐ¾ Ð½Ð° Ð¿ÑÐ°Ð²Ð¾ÑÐ¾. ÐÑÑÐ¸Ð½ÑÐºÐ°ÑÐ° Ð·Ð°ÑÐ¸ÑÐ° Ð½Ð° Ð´ÐµÐ¼Ð¾ÐºÑÐ°ÑÐ¸ÑÑÐ° Ð½Ðµ Ðµ Ð² ÑÐ¾Ð²Ð° Ð´Ð° ÑÐµ ÑÑÐ·Ð´Ð°Ð²Ð°Ñ ÑÑÐ¼Ð½ÐµÐ½Ð¸Ñ, Ð° Ð² ÑÐ¾Ð²Ð° Ð´Ð° ÑÐµ Ð·Ð°ÑÐ¸ÑÐ°Ð²Ð° Ð¿Ð¾ÑÑÐ¸Ð³Ð½Ð°ÑÐ¾ÑÐ¾. ÐÐ°ÑÐ°ÑÐ° ÑÐµÐ» ÑÑÑÐ±Ð²Ð° Ð´Ð° Ð±ÑÐ´Ðµ Ð´Ð° Ð¿Ð¾Ð´ÐºÑÐµÐ¿ÑÐ¼Ðµ Ð¿Ð¾ÑÑÐ¸Ð³Ð½Ð°ÑÐ¸Ñ Ð½Ð°Ð¿ÑÐµÐ´ÑÐº, Ð·Ð° Ð´Ð° Ð¼Ð¾Ð¶Ðµ ÑÐ¾Ð¹ Ð´Ð° Ð¿ÑÐ¾Ð´ÑÐ»Ð¶Ð¸, Ð·Ð°ÑÐ¾ÑÐ¾ ÑÑÐ°Ð±Ð¸Ð»Ð½Ð° ÐÑÐ»Ð³Ð°ÑÐ¸Ñ Ð¾Ð·Ð½Ð°ÑÐ°Ð²Ð° Ð¿Ð¾-ÑÐ¸Ð»ÐµÐ½ ÐÐ²ÑÐ¾Ð¿ÐµÐ¹ÑÐºÐ¸ ÑÑÑÐ·.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Svenja Hahn (Renew). â MadamÂ President, colleagues, we see democracy slipping through our fingers in Bulgaria. The EU is on the brink of losing yet another country to state capture, and I'm shocked by the blind eye the colleagues from the EPP and S&D are turning on this.

According to civil society reports, key institutions â like the judiciary, security services, parts of the media â are under the influence of a Magnitsky-sanctioned oligarch, Delyan Peevski, with former Prime Minister Borisov and his GERB party allegedly acting as the political front.

And while the EPP â his political family â looks away, the ALDE party acted. When Peevski took over our former Bulgarian member party, literally that same day the ALDE bureau decided to end their membership. Because defending democracy means taking action, not making excuses. So when will the EPP take action?

The arrest of the MayorÂ of Varna is the clearest sign yet that the rule of law is failing. A democratically elected mayor detained without due process is a signal to everyone in Bulgaria: stay silent or face consequences.

Bulgarian civil society calls on Europe, and I call on the European Commission: act now. Send a fact-finding mission, investigate the case of Mayor Kotsev and be ready to freeze EU funds if needed â because if Bulgaria loses democracy, Europe will lose Bulgaria.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Juan Ignacio Zoido Ãlvarez (PPE). â SeÃ±ora presidenta, seÃ±or comisario, como juez de carrera siempre he defendido que la separaciÃ³n de poderes no es una mera formalidad, sino un principio inviolable. Es el pilar que sostiene nuestras democracias y la garantÃ­a Ãºltima de que la justicia actÃºa con independencia, sin presiones polÃ­ticas ni partidistas. Tiene que ser independiente.

Por eso debemos reconocer los avances que Bulgaria ha tenido en esta materia. El Ãºltimo informe sobre el Estado de Derecho de la ComisiÃ³n Europea destaca mejoras en su independencia judicial, en la rendiciÃ³n de cuentas del Fiscal General y el esfuerzo del Consejo Judicial Supremo y de los mecanismos de la lucha contra la corrupciÃ³n. Y habrÃ¡ que seguir adelante.

Gracias a las polÃ­ticas responsables del GERB y de Boiko BorÃ­sov, Bulgaria hoy forma parte ya de Schengen y en los prÃ³ximos meses se incorporarÃ¡ a la zona del euro. Son logros que refuerzan un compromiso europeo y una estabilidad institucional. Ni este Parlamento ni el Gobierno nacional deben interferir en los procesos judiciales en curso, como, por ejemplo, el del alcalde de Varna.

Defender el Estado de Derecho no consiste en dictar sentencia desde esta tribuna, sino en garantizar que los jueces puedan dictarlas libres de toda presiÃ³n.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Ð¥ÑÐ¸ÑÑÐ¾ ÐÐµÑÑÐ¾Ð² (Renew). â Ð-Ð¶Ð¾ ÐÑÐµÐ´ÑÐµÐ´Ð°ÑÐµÐ», ÑÐ²Ð°Ð¶Ð°ÐµÐ¼Ð¸ ÐºÐ¾Ð»ÐµÐ³Ð¸, ÑÐ²Ð°Ð¶Ð°ÐµÐ¼Ð¸ Ð±ÑÐ»Ð³Ð°ÑÐ¸, ÑÐ¸ÑÑÐ°ÑÐ¸ÑÑÐ° Ðµ Ð¿ÑÐµÐ´ÐµÐ»Ð½Ð¾ ÑÑÐ½Ð° Ð¸ Ð½Ðµ Ð¾Ñ Ð²ÑÐµÑÐ°. ÐÑÐµÐºÐ¸ ÐµÐ´Ð¸Ð½ ÑÐ¾Ð²ÐµÐº Ð² ÐÑÐ»Ð³Ð°ÑÐ¸Ñ Ð·Ð½Ð°Ðµ Ð·Ð° ÐºÐ°ÐºÐ²Ð¾ ÑÑÐ°Ð²Ð° Ð´ÑÐ¼Ð°. ÐÑÐµÐºÐ¸ ÐµÐ´Ð¸Ð½ Ð¾Ñ ÑÐ¾ÑÐ°ÑÐ°, ÐºÐ¾Ð¸ÑÐ¾ Ð²Ð·ÐµÐ¼Ð°Ñ ÑÐµÑÐµÐ½Ð¸Ñ Ð² ÐÐ²ÑÐ¾Ð¿ÐµÐ¹ÑÐºÐ°ÑÐ° ÐºÐ¾Ð¼Ð¸ÑÐ¸Ñ, ÑÑÑÐ¾ Ð·Ð½Ð°Ðµ Ð·Ð° ÐºÐ°ÐºÐ²Ð¾ ÑÑÐ°Ð²Ð° Ð´ÑÐ¼Ð° Ð¸ ÑÐµÐ·ÑÐ»ÑÐ°ÑÐ¸ÑÐµ ÑÐ° Ð²Ð¸Ð´Ð¸Ð¼Ð¸. ÐÑÐ»Ð±Ð¾Ðº Ð¿Ð¾ÐºÐ»Ð¾Ð½ ÐºÑÐ¼ ÑÐµÐ·Ð¸ Ð¼Ð¾Ð¸ ÑÑÐ³ÑÐ°Ð¶Ð´Ð°Ð½Ð¸, ÐºÐ¾Ð¸ÑÐ¾ Ð¾Ð¿Ð¸ÑÐ²Ð°Ñ ÑÑÑ ÑÐ¾Ð±ÑÑÐ²ÐµÐ½Ð¸ÑÐµ ÑÐ¸ ÑÑÑÐµ Ð´Ð° ÑÐµÑÐ°Ñ Ð¿ÑÐ¾Ð±Ð»ÐµÐ¼Ð° ÑÑÑ ÑÐ¾ÑÐ¸Ð¹ÑÐºÐ¸Ñ Ð±Ð¾ÐºÐ»ÑÐº. Ð Ð´ÑÐ»Ð±Ð¾Ðº Ð¿Ð¾ÐºÐ»Ð¾Ð½ ÐºÑÐ¼ Ð²ÑÐµÐºÐ¸ ÐµÐ´Ð¸Ð½ Ð±ÑÐ»Ð³Ð°ÑÐ¸Ð½, ÐºÐ¾Ð¹ÑÐ¾ Ðµ Ð¾ÑÑÐ·Ð½Ð°Ð», ÑÐµ Ð½ÑÐ¼Ð° ÐºÐ°Ðº Ð¶Ð¸Ð²Ð¾ÑÑÑ Ð´Ð° ÑÐµ Ð¶Ð¸Ð²ÐµÐµ Ð² ÑÐ°ÐºÐ°Ð»Ð½ÑÑÐ° Ð½Ð° Ð¶Ð¸Ð²Ð¾ÑÐ°.

ÐÐ½ÐµÑÐ½Ð¸ÑÑ Ð´ÐµÐ½ Ð·Ð° Ð½Ð°Ñ Ðµ Ð½Ð°ÑÑÐ¾ÑÑÐµ, Ð½Ð¾ ÐµÐ´Ð¸Ð½ Ð´ÐµÐ½ ÑÐ¾Ð¹ ÑÐµ Ð±ÑÐ´Ðµ Ð² Ð¸ÑÑÐ¾ÑÐ¸ÑÑÐ° Ð¸ Ð² ÑÑÐµÐ±Ð½Ð¸ÐºÐ° Ð¿Ð¾ Ð¸ÑÑÐ¾ÑÐ¸Ñ Ð·Ð° Ð´Ð½ÐµÑÐ½Ð¸Ñ Ð´ÐµÐ½ ÑÐµ Ð¿Ð¸ÑÐµ: âÐÐ»Ð°Ð³Ð¾Ð¼Ð¸Ñ ÐÐ¾ÑÐµÐ² Ð±ÐµÑÐµ ÑÐ²Ð¾Ð±Ð¾Ð´ÐµÐ½, Ð° ÑÐµÐ´ÐµÐ¼ Ð¼Ð¸Ð»Ð¸Ð¾Ð½Ð° Ð±ÑÑÐ° Ð² Ð°ÑÐµÑÑÐ°â.

Â 
Â Â 
Â Â 

PRÃSIDENCE: YOUNOUS OMARJEE
Vice-PrÃ©sident

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â MichaÅ Wawrykiewicz (PPE). â Mr President, colleagues, the rule of law remains a cornerstone of our Union, and I hope we are all fully convinced of this. In recent years, this has been reaffirmed through the jurisprudence of the Court of Justice of the European Union, as well as through efforts of the European Commission and the European Parliament.

The Union has developed a number of instruments to follow and assess developments in this field. Among them, the rule of law report offers a valuable overview of the situation in each Member State, acknowledging That no country is entirely free from challenges.

I am fully confident that Bulgaria, as a member of the European family and as a country deeply attached to our shared values, as shown, among others, through its consistent support for Ukraine in the face of Russian aggression and through membership in Schengen, and soon in eurozone, will continue on its path of commitment to democracy, justice and the rule of law and the full implementation of all recommendations of the European Commission.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Dan JÃ¸rgensen, Member of the Commission. â Mr President, honourable Members, thank you for this interesting debate and thank you for sharing your views on the situation of the rule of law in Bulgaria.

The annual rule of law report has become a key preventive instrument to improve the rule of law situation in the EU, including in Bulgaria. However, it is one of the instruments at our disposal. The Commission remains committed to using all the instruments at its disposal to defend the rule of law, in order to provide an effective and proportionate response to any interference with the rule of law. We continue working with national authorities to help them with the implementation of the report recommendation. We also remain fully committed to a dialogue on this matter. The Commission looks forward to continuing to exchange with this Parliament and with the Council, national parliaments and civil society on the annual rule of law reports, including on the country chapters.

More generally, let me also flag two upcoming initiatives of crucial importance to our overall democratic systems. The European democracy shield will put forward actions to promote, protect and strengthen our democracies in the long run. It will be accompanied by a civil society strategy, recognising the key role of civil society organisations in safeguarding and promoting EU values in stronger and more resilient democracies.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Marie Bjerre, President-in-Office of the Council. â Mr President, honourable Members, Commissioner, thank you for this debate, and also thank you for your insights on the situation in Bulgaria. Let me assure you that the Council remains committed to promoting all available tools to ensure respect for the rule of law. We will continue to pay close attention to any new developments in this area. Finally, we should not forget that it is in our shared responsibility â Member States and EU institutions together â to uphold the rule of law and fundamental values.

Â 

15. Presentation of the Court of Auditors' annual report 2024 (debate)
Video of the speeches
Â 

Â Â Le PrÃ©sident. â L'ordre du jour appelle Ã  prÃ©sent le dÃ©bat sur la prÃ©sentation du rapport annuel 2024 de la Cour des comptes europÃ©enne.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Tony Murphy, President of the Court of Auditors. â MonsieurÂ President, honourable Members, Commissioner, I am very pleased to be here today to present the findings and core messages from the European Court of Auditors' annual report for 2024. I am once again accompanied by my colleague Jan Gregor, who oversees the production of this report.

I think this year's report is highly relevant as negotiations for the 2028-2034 MFF are under way. I think it offers important lessons and highlights risks that should be considered when shaping future budgets.

The key takeaways from the report: as in previous years, we provide a clean opinion on the reliability of the EU accounts and on the EU's budget revenue. Regarding the regularity of expenditure, we offer two separate opinions: oneÂ for the traditional EU budget and one for the RRF, reflecting the differentÂ nature of these types of spending.

Starting with the traditional EU budget spending, here, for 2024, our estimated error rate is 3.6Â %, down from 5.6Â % in 2023, meaning it remains material. Given the pervasiveness of the errors identified, we have given an adverse opinion for the sixth consecutive year. The cohesion policy area continues to be the main source that feeds our overall error rate, and it is important to reiterate that for this year, the error rate for cohesion is 5.7Â %, down from 9.3Â % in 2023. I think in 2023, issues such as pandemic disruptions and time pressure to use 100Â % co-financing schemes likely increased irregular spending risks in this area. Consequently, in 2024, the cohesion error rate has returned to levels which were more consistent with previous years.

To summarise our compliance work on the 2024 MFF budget expenditure, while the estimated level of error in spending has decreased, it remains a concern.Â I want to make it very clear here today why it is concerning. As I mentioned, the main source of our error stems from cohesion funding. Our audit approach here is based on transactions already reviewed by Member States' audit authorities and/or the Commission, which in theory should be error-free after their checks. Yet we continue to find errors that these auditors should have detected and did not, and this has been an ongoing issue for the last number of years. The persistent failure to detect these errors raises serious questions about the adequacy and effectiveness of oversight and control mechanisms in Member States.

Most concerning is that, in many cases, the same control structures and bodies responsible for cohesion funding are also responsible for checking RRF funds, so similar such errors are likely to present there. However, checks for such errors are not performed when making payments under the RRF. In our view, this lack of oversight undermines assurance and accountability, posing a serious risk to the EU's financial interests.

Indeed, this concern is echoed by EPPO's 2024 annual report, which revealed 307 active funding fraud investigations related to the RRF in EU Member States, making it the third largest in volume across the EU programmes, with cases likely to grow.

It's in this context that we must avoid repeating past mistakes. We must prioritise accountability and transparency as guiding principles in the discussions and development of the next MFF. This is especially important, given that the proposed national plans are very similar in nature and design to the RRF.

Concerning the regularity of the 2024 RRF expenditure, we issued a qualified opinion. As under the RRF, payments are not linked to actual costs, our assessment is limited to the payment conditions set out in the RRF Regulation; that is, that milestones and targets must be satisfactorily fulfilled. Therefore, our opinion for RRF expenditure is not comparable to the one we give for the traditional EU budget expenditure.

This year, we again found milestones and targets linked to payments which were not satisfactorily fulfilled, we noted issues related to the eligibility periods, and we also drew attention to the Court of Justice case concerning two super milestones. We know that there is an increasing trend in the overall financial impact of the quantitative findings. In terms of disbursement, to date, disbursements have totalled EURÂ 225 billion, just over 60Â % of the total funds in terms of grants, with less than a year remaining before the AugustÂ 2026 deadline. This means significant expenditure still needs to be spent, with nearly 4Â 200 milestones and targets still to be assessed within just ten months.

Additionally, this year we also published a review on the RRF, consolidating our extensive work on the facility, highlighting the key findings and lessons for future performance-based instruments. We emphasise that while such instruments can be of value, they must only be used when funding links directly to measurable results, with clear traceability to actual costs. It is essential that their design does not compromise accountability.

Looking ahead, our annual report also highlights risks from borrowing for future MFFs, mainly due to the increasing debt obligations of NGEU. By 2027, EU borrowing could exceed EURÂ 900 billion, nearly ten times the pre-NGEU level. Interest costs could surpass EURÂ 30 billion for the current MFF â more than double the initial forecasts. Debt repayments are deferred to future budgets, posing serious challenges to safeguarding those future budgets and ensuring that sufficient resources are available for EU actions. In fact, the proposed 2 trillion budget for 2028-2034 includes an allocation for debt repayment, which could cost up to EURÂ 31 billion per annum.

Thank you for your attention.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Piotr Serafin, Member of the Commission. â Mr President, dear President of the European Court of Auditors, let me start by saying that the Commission welcomes the report and appreciates the recommendations of the court.

The sound management of the EU budget is our common objective. Of course, I am pleased to note that the court has provided an unqualified opinion on the EU accounts in 2024 that shows that our accounts are complete, transparent and reliable, covering all assets and liabilities, including our borrowing activities.

As in previous years, the court has also concluded that revenue transactions and administrative expenditure remain free from material error. These are positive developments. On the other hand, I acknowledge that the court has maintained its adverse opinion on expenditure and has provided a qualified opinion on the Recovery and Resilience Facility for 2024.

The court's estimated level of error is significantly lower than in 2023 and in 2022, but it is not low enough. Therefore, I want to assure you that the Commission is committed to bringing the level of error down further.

Let me underline that our assessment largely converges with the one from the court. We agree with the court on the spending areas where the risk of errors is high, namely those with complex rules. Some methodological discrepancies with the ECA's assessment remain. Nonetheless, we largely agree with the court on the root causes of errors identified.Â We will continue to work with the court on fostering common understanding and reduce divergences in views.

Ladies and gentlemen, the Commission is committed to bring the level of error down further and to implement the recommendations of the court. They help us to improve the Commission's management and control systems. Let me conclude by saying that the Commission has accepted or partially accepted all but one of the court's recommendations expressed in this year's report, and that we also take seriously into account your views with respect to the next Multiannual Financial Framework.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Niclas Herbst, im Namen der PPE-Fraktion. â Herr PrÃ¤sident, lieber Herr Kommissar, liebe Kolleginnen und Kollegen! Ich freue mich natÃ¼rlich besondersÂ â und heiÃe ihn auch herzlich willkommenÂ â Ã¼ber Tony Murphy, aber auch Ã¼ber Jan Gregor und alle anderen vom EuropÃ¤ischen Rechnungshof, weil wir Ã¼ber die vergangenen Monate hervorragend zusammengearbeitet haben. DafÃ¼r mÃ¶chte ich mich sehr herzlich bedanken. Ich glaube, Sie haben mit dem Jahresbericht 2024 eine gute Vorlage fÃ¼r unsere Arbeit geliefert. Auch dafÃ¼r ein herzliches DankeschÃ¶n.

NatÃ¼rlich reden wir Ã¼ber die Fehlerquote. Die Fehlerquote ist beileibe nicht alles, aber sie ist natÃ¼rlich ein wichtiger Indikator. Ich freue mich darÃ¼ber, sehr geehrter Herr Kommissar, dass Sie hier auch den Handlungsbedarf beschrieben haben. Wir brauchen dringend Reformen, um die Fehlerquote weiter zu senken. Es gibt zu viele Fehler bei den EUâAusgaben, und der Rechnungshof warnt, dass anhaltende Fehler und wachsende Schuldenverpflichtungen langfristig die finanzielle Nachhaltigkeit der Union gefÃ¤hrden kÃ¶nnten. In dem Jahresbericht bestÃ¤tigt der Rechnungshof, dass das Gesamtniveau der Fehler â Sie haben es schon gehÃ¶rt â bei den Ausgaben zwar gesunken ist, aber weiterhin Ã¼ber den akzeptablen Grenzen liegt. Und richtig ist auch â das hat der PrÃ¤sident des Rechnungshofes dargestellt â, das sind alles Fehler, die bereits hÃ¤tten entdeckt werden mÃ¼ssen.

Wenn wir auch die VerlÃ¤sslichkeit der nationalen Audit-Systeme weiterdenken fÃ¼r den nÃ¤chsten MFR und die Ideen, die es dort gibt, dann gibt uns das Anlass zur Sorge. Es gibt eine anhaltende Diskussion Ã¼ber die Berechnungsmethode â die haben wir immerÂ â, und natÃ¼rlich hoffen wir als Parlament, dass es bald einheitliche Berechnungsmethoden geben sollÂ â ich will das zur VollstÃ¤ndigkeit hier gerne sagen â zur ARF. Wir brauchen natÃ¼rlich Verbesserungen, wir brauchen mehr FlexibilitÃ¤t bei der Gestaltung zukÃ¼nftiger leistungsbasierter Instrumente. Auch 2024 stellt allerdings der Rechnungshof fest, dass die Kommission Zahlungen geleistet hat, obwohl Targets und Milestones nur unzureichend erfÃ¼llt waren. Diese Mittel hÃ¤tten nicht ausgezahlt werden dÃ¼rfen.

DarÃ¼ber hinaus gibt es bei der Umsetzung der ARF Probleme bei der Mittelbindung undÂ â was am gravierendsten istÂ â einen Mangel an Transparenz. Ich sage sehr deutlich: NatÃ¼rlich dÃ¼rfen diese Probleme beim zukÃ¼nftigen mehrjÃ¤hrigen Finanzrahmen nicht auftreten. Die RÃ¼ckverfolgbarkeitÂ â traceabilityÂ â der ARF-Mittel ist ebenfalls genannt worden. Das macht uns groÃe Sorgen. Und eine zentrale Mahnung des Rechnungshofes lautet: Solche Instrumente sollten nur eingesetzt werden, wenn die Finanzierung direkt mit messbaren Ergebnissen verknÃ¼pft und nachvollziehbar auf tatsÃ¤chliche Kosten zurÃ¼ckgefÃ¼hrt werden kann. Darauf hoffen wir auch beim nÃ¤chsten mehrjÃ¤hrigen Finanzrahmen.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Carla Tavares, em nome do Grupo S&D. â Senhor Presidente, Senhor ComissÃ¡rio e Senhor Presidente do Tribunal de Contas Europeu, o relatÃ³rio anual do Tribunal de Contas Ã© sempre uma oportunidade para avaliarmos o que fizemos e o que podemos melhorar no futuro em termos orÃ§amentais da UniÃ£o.

Â Â Joachim StanisÅaw BrudziÅski, w imieniu grupy ECR. â Panie PrzewodniczÄcy! Oznacza to, Å¼e nie jest moÅ¼liwe peÅne potwierdzenie prawidÅowoÅci wydatkÃ³w ostatecznych beneficjentÃ³w.

Najlepszym potwierdzeniem sÅusznoÅci spostrzeÅ¼eÅ TrybunaÅu moÅ¼e byÄ POLITYCZNE odblokowanie ÅrodkÃ³w z KPO dla Polski wraz z ostatnimi doniesieniami o przyznaniu tych ÅrodkÃ³w wÄtpliwie dobranym beneficjentom. PrzykÅad: klub dla swingersÃ³w. To nie jest Å¼art. Klub dla swingersÃ³w, jachty z przeznaczeniem na inwestycje o nierokujÄcym przeznaczeniu i ekonomicznej nieopÅacalnoÅci. Problemy z monitorowaniem wydatkÃ³w na transformacjÄ klimatycznÄ, nieprawidÅowoÅci przy zakupach szczepionek przeciw COVID-19 rodzÄ ryzyko dalszych naduÅ¼yÄ, nieefektywnego wydatkowania publicznych ÅrodkÃ³w oraz potwierdzajÄ podwÃ³jne standardy Komisji Europejskiej, ktÃ³ra nie stosuje siÄ do zasady transparentnoÅci finansÃ³w publicznych, elementarnej w paÅstwach prawa, ktÃ³re sama chce przy tym kontrolowaÄ.

Â Â Olivier Chastel, au nom du groupe Renew. â Monsieur le PrÃ©sident, Monsieur le PrÃ©sident de la Cour des comptes, Monsieur le Commissaire, chers collÃ¨gues, le rapport annuel de la Cour des comptes a confirmÃ© l'ensemble de nos prÃ©occupations: un taux d'erreur global Ã  3,6Â %, et mÃªme 5,7Â % pour les fonds de cohÃ©sion. Les annÃ©es passent et les problÃ¨mes demeurent. En plus, nous savons que la Commission ambitionne d'importer le modÃ¨le de la facilitÃ© pour la reprise et la rÃ©silience (FRR) dans le cadre financier pluriannuel. LÃ  aussi, la plus grande prudence s'impose.

Si le modÃ¨le FRR a pu apporter une certaine flexibilitÃ©, de nombreux manquements sont aussi Ã  dÃ©plorer, notamment en matiÃ¨re de dÃ©tection des erreurs, des fraudes au niveau national, ainsi d'ailleurs que dans la dÃ©finition souvent peu claire des jalons Ã  atteindre. Plus que jamais, nous avons besoin d'Ãªtre attentifs Ã  la mise en place d'un cadre rÃ©glementaire efficace, notamment via une numÃ©risation systÃ©matisÃ©e des processus, des contrÃ´les, l'utilisation de l'exploration de donnÃ©es et une meilleure coopÃ©ration avec les Ãtats membres.

Â 
Â Â

Â Â Daniel Freund, on behalf of the Verts/ALE Group. â Mr President, dear colleagues, hidden behind the diplomatic language of the 557 pages of this report, I think there are some very serious problems identified by the Court of Auditors, particularly regarding the EURÂ 650Â billion post-COVID recovery fund. Actually, the report says it's not clear what citizens actually get for their money. I was very much in favour of this fund, but I â and we all, here in Parliament â we wanted to see who is actually getting this money, and we wrote it into the regulation that we want to see the 100 largest beneficiaries, but we don't get to see them.

Member States tell us that the biggest beneficiaries of this fund were all kinds of government agencies, ministries, in all Member States. It's the same, pretty much across the spectrum. So either we just plugged some budget holes in some ministries, which I don't think was the objective, but I also don't think it's what happened. It's that Member States blatantly ignore the legislation, and that is something, Commissioner, that you need to fix. That needs to be addressed, particularly if you want to convince this House that we spend 53.7Â % of the next budget under the same functioning. We need to see where this money went.

Â 
Â Â 
Â

Det Ã¤r i grunden en oansvarig politik, som gÃ¶r EU mer kÃ¤nsligt bÃ¥de fÃ¶r rÃ¤ntesvÃ¤ngningarna och fÃ¶r vilken rating man har. NÃ¤sta generations EU har blivit att nÃ¤sta generation betalar.

NÃ¤r det gÃ¤ller Ã¥terhÃ¤mtningsfonden pÃ¥minner den om morgonen efter en rejÃ¤l krogrunda nÃ¤r man har besÃ¶kt lite fÃ¶r mÃ¥nga barer, nÃ¤r man tittar pÃ¥ kreditkortet och inte riktigt vet vad man anvÃ¤nde pengarna till kvÃ¤llen fÃ¶re. Men man inser: âOj, nu finns det inte mycket kvar att leva pÃ¥ framÃ¶verâ.

Vi kan inte hantera skattebetalarnas pengar pÃ¥ det sÃ¤ttet. Vi mÃ¥ste veta vart de har gÃ¥tt. Vi mÃ¥ste veta vilken nytta de gÃ¶r. Och som ordfÃ¶rande Murphy mycket riktigt pÃ¥pekade: Vi mÃ¥ste lÃ¤ra oss av vÃ¥ra misstag och inte gÃ¶ra om dem i nÃ¤sta flerÃ¥riga budget.

Â Â Julien Sanchez (PfE). â Monsieur le PrÃ©sident, Monsieur le Commissaire, je voudrais d'abord remercier la Cour des comptes europÃ©enne. Elle dresse un bilan alarmant de la gestion du budget de l'Union europÃ©enne, un taux d'erreur global de 3,6Â % dans les dÃ©penses en 2024. Si l'Union europÃ©enne Ã©tait une entreprise, son Ã©quipe dirigeante serait dÃ©barquÃ©e.

S'agissant des fonds de la facilitÃ© pour la reprise et la rÃ©silience, la Cour des comptes Ã©met une opinion avec rÃ©serves sur la lÃ©galitÃ© et la rÃ©gularitÃ© des dÃ©penses, dÃ©nonÃ§ant des jalons imprÃ©cis, des Ã©valuations arbitraires de la Commission et des contrÃ´les nationaux insuffisants. Elle documente Ã©galement des violations de conditions d'Ã©ligibilitÃ©, des annulations, un cas de double financement, ainsi qu'une traÃ§abilitÃ© toujours lacunaire des fonds dans plusieurs Ãtats membres.

Quant Ã  NextGeneration, le constat est tout aussi alarmant, avec des paiements qui ont dÃ©passÃ© les engagements de prÃ¨s de 50Â milliards d'euros, traduisant une absence totale de discipline financiÃ¨re.

Alors cette lÃ©gÃ¨retÃ© n'est plus une erreur, c'est une mÃ©thode. L'Union dÃ©pense sans contrÃ´le, sans transparence, sans rendre de comptes. Je rappelle qu'on parle ici d'instruments reprÃ©sentant des centaines de milliards d'euros.

DÃ©signÃ© par ce Parlement rapporteur pour le rapport sur la protection des intÃ©rÃªts financiers 2024, j'aurai Ã  cÅur de travailler avec chacun pour proposer des solutions pour lutter contre la fraude, la criminalitÃ© organisÃ©e, les sous-dÃ©clarations. Au lieu de crÃ©er de nouveaux impÃ´ts ou des ressources propres, allons chercher l'argent qu'on nous doit et traquons les fraudeurs.

Â Â

Â Â Gerben-Jan Gerbrandy (Renew). â Mr President, President Murphy, Commissioner, dear colleagues, this year's annual report is not just another audit document to tick off, it's a political compass, and it shows us how European taxpayers' money is being spent and, more importantly, whether it truly delivers value for our citizens. And this year, it carries even more weight because we are entering the negotiations for the next MFF. And the choices we make now will define Europe's priorities for the years to come.

If there's one thing clear, it's that we cannot repeat the mistakes of the RRF, many colleagues already said that. Simply throwing money on the table without proper direction or safeguards is not strategy. Funding must be targeted, protected and aligned with our common goals. Our already tiny European budget is far too precious to become a blank cheque for our Member States.

Â 
Â Â 
Â

Poziom bÅÄdÃ³w znaczÄco spadÅ z roku na rok, ale nadal wedÅug ETO jest niepokojÄcy. Podzielam oczywiÅcie ten poglÄd. Natomiast najwaÅ¼niejsze jest, skÄd biorÄ siÄ te nieprawidÅowoÅci, a mianowicie niekwalifikowane koszty i projekty, naruszanie procedur zamÃ³wieÅ publicznych, brak dokumentÃ³w potwierdzajÄcych wydatki. Co Åwiadczy o tym, Å¼e potrzebujemy prawdziwego systemu uproszczeÅ wzglÄdem wdraÅ¼ania ÅrodkÃ³w europejskich. Uproszczenia przede wszystkim.

I na koniec jeszcze jeden cytat, Panie PrzewodniczÄcy. âTrybunaÅ uznaje, Å¼e RRF rozmyÅ mechanizmy odpowiedzialnoÅci, ktÃ³re w polityce spÃ³jnoÅci sÄ bardziej ustrukturyzowaneâ. To wniosek na przyszÅoÅÄ przy dyskusji nad nowym budÅ¼etem.

Â Â

Â Â JosÃ© Cepeda (S&D). â SeÃ±or presidente, seÃ±or presidente Murphy, muchas gracias por el informe anual del Tribunal de Cuentas Europeo.

Sin lugar a dudas este informe representa un ejercicio esencial de control democrÃ¡tico sobre el uso del presupuesto de la UniÃ³n y, en ese sentido, como ponente socialista, en estas instituciones, destacar que el Tribunal de hecho no formula observaciones a las cuentas del Tribunal de Justicia, del Defensor del Pueblo Europeo ni del Supervisor Europeo de ProtecciÃ³n de Datos, lo que demuestra una gestiÃ³n sÃ³lida y transparente de estas instituciones.

Sin embargo hay una cosa que a mÃ­ sÃ­ que me preocupa de una forma especial âen lo que vengo trabajando en este Parlamento en los Ãºltimos tiemposâ y que, ademÃ¡s, forma parte de la esencia de los debates estructurales, cada vez mÃ¡s presentes, que tiene mucho que ver con la seguridad y la defensa, donde advierte el informe de una gestiÃ³n y una ejecuciÃ³n lenta y fragmentada. Teniendo en cuenta la implosiÃ³n presupuestaria que estamos teniendo, sobre todo de cara al prÃ³ximo presupuesto a largo plazo, que prÃ¡cticamente multiplica por diez la inversiÃ³n en seguridad y defensa, me gustarÃ­a que trabajÃ¡ramos mucho mÃ¡s tambiÃ©n para que ni un solo gramo, ni un solo euro quedara deslegitimado.

No se trata solo de gastar mÃ¡s en defensa sino tambiÃ©n de hacerlo mÃ¡s con inteligencia, transparencia y visiÃ³n europea.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Bert-Jan Ruissen (ECR). â Voorzitter, collega's, de Europese Rekenkamer legt ook dit keer feilloos de vinger op een aantal zere plekken: de enorme schuldenberg (900 miljard EUR tegen 2027), de stijgende rentelasten (30Â miljard EUR per jaar), maar ook het foutenpercentage dat nog steeds te hoog is en het zeer gebrekkige toezicht op de herstel- en veerkrachtfaciliteit (RRF). Vaak is niet eens bekend wie de eindbegunstigden zijn en wordt zelfs geen rekening gehouden met de werkelijk gemaakte kosten, maar slechts met vage mijlpalen en streefdoelen.

Mijn oproep vandaag is dan ook: laten we stoppen met het aangaan van nieuwe leningen, laten we het financiÃ«le toezicht aanzienlijk aanscherpen en laten we ook met elkaar vaststellen dat de performance-based aanpak in de huidige vorm onder het RRF niet als model kan dienen voor het nieuwe meerjarig financieel kader (MFK). Deze aanpak zal grondig op de schop moeten. De belastingbetaler verdient beter.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Gilles Boyer (Renew). â Monsieur le PrÃ©sident, Monsieur le PrÃ©sident de la Cour des comptes europÃ©enne, Monsieur le Commissaire, le rapport de la Cour des comptes illustre la nÃ©cessitÃ© d'apporter plus de simplification et de flexibilitÃ© au prochain cadre financier pluriannuel, sans renoncer, bien sÃ»r, Ã  l'exigence de transparence et de traÃ§abilitÃ© de chaque euro public dÃ©pensÃ©.

La Cour des comptes le souligne dans les instruments qui sont fondÃ©s sur la performance, tels que la facilitÃ© pour la reprise et la rÃ©silience. Les contrÃ´les reposent majoritairement sur les Ãtats membres, dont les Ã©valuations manquent parfois de fiabilitÃ©. Nous nous heurtons donc souvent Ã  l'impossibilitÃ© de mesurer les rÃ©sultats concrets de l'utilisation et donc de l'efficacitÃ© des fonds europÃ©ens. Or, c'est lÃ -dessus que nous serons collectivement jugÃ©s, ici au Parlement, mes chers collÃ¨gues, mais aussi Ã  la Commission. Monsieur le Commissaire, c'est donc, je crois, l'unique objectif que nous devons nous fixer pour l'avenir, avec l'aide de la Cour des comptes.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Caterina Chinnici (PPE). â Signor Presidente, signor Commissario, signor Presidente della Corte dei conti europea, onorevoli colleghi, soprattutto oggi, nell'attuale contesto, con un ammontare senza precedenti di risorse stanziate, il sovrapporsi di strumenti di finanziamento differenti, il susseguirsi di incertezze geopolitiche e l'elaborazione del nuovo QFP, la relazione annuale della Corte dei conti assume un'importanza decisiva per le preziose indicazioni che dobbiamo trarne.

Il tasso di errore delle spese tradizionali â in calo certo ma ancora rilevante â e la stretta relazione tra complessitÃ  delle procedure e rischio di errore ci dicono che dobbiamo fare di piÃ¹ per la semplificazione e quindi applicare correttamente le novitÃ  che sul punto introduce il nuovo regolamento finanziario.

Vanno al tempo stesso rafforzati e migliorati i controlli, cosÃ¬ come il lavoro delle autoritÃ  di audit, e occorre accelerare l'assorbimento e l'esecuzione dei fondi, soprattutto nella politica di coesione, anche sostenendo le amministrazioni locali.

Quanto all'RRF, senza dimenticare il contesto in cui Ã¨ stato varato e i benefici che sin qui ha prodotto, i rilievi della Corte su definizione e verifica di traguardi e obiettivi, tracciabilitÃ  di fondi, controlli interni e tutela degli interessi finanziari dell'UE devono costituire un monito anche per i futuri programmi europei.

Ã nostro dovere assicurare che il bilancio dell'Unione sia speso bene, senza ritardi, errori, frodi e interferenze illecite. E per questo il lavoro della Corte dei conti, cui va il nostro ringraziamento, rappresenta un riferimento fondamentale.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Johan Van Overtveldt (ECR). â Voorzitter, commissaris, president, collega's, rapporten zoals die van de Europese Rekenkamer zijn, net als die van bijvoorbeeld het Europees Openbaar Ministerie, geen vrijblijvende stapels papier. Het werk van deze onafhankelijke controleorganen blijft naar mijn mening te vaak onderbelicht en blijft helaas ook te vaak onbeantwoord.

De Rekenkamer geeft in haar rapport nogmaals onomwonden aan dat met name de aard en de uitgaven van het Coronafonds, het RRF, soms problematisch zijn. De controles brachten gevallen aan het licht waarbij mijlpalen en doelstellingen die aan betalingen waren gekoppeld, niet naar behoren waren gerealiseerd. Ook werden bijvoorbeeld gevallen van dubbele financiering vastgesteld. De Rekenkamer signaleert ook de zwaktes in de ex ante beoordelingen van de Commissie.

Ik onderschrijf dan ook ten volle de oproep van de Commissie. Dergelijke instrumenten mogen enkel worden ingezet wanneer de financiering rechtstreeks gekoppeld is aan meetbare resultaten. Op dat vlak vormt het RRF geen voorbeeld voor de opmaak van het volgende MFK. Er moet een veel sterkere focus komen op prestatiemeting, transparantie en verantwoording.

Â 
Â Â 
Â

OrbÃ¡nÃ©k ugyanis nem azzal foglalkoznak, hogy jÃ³l kormÃ¡nyozzanak, hanem hogy hogyan tudjÃ¡k mÃ©g gazdagabbÃ¡ tenni a barÃ¡taikat, minÃ©l jobban megtÃ¶mni a sajÃ¡t zsebÃ¼ket. Â Â Sebastian Tynkkynen (ECR). â Arvoisa puhemies, no nyt saatiin lukuja pÃ¶ytÃ¤Ã¤n. Â Â Isabel Benjumea Benjumea (PPE). â SeÃ±or presidente, seÃ±or comisario, quiero comenzar agradeciendo y poniendo en valor el trabajo del Tribunal de Cuentas Europeo. Sus informes y evaluaciones han sido, y siguen siendo, imprescindibles para arrojar luz sobre la gestiÃ³n de los fondos europeos por parte de la ComisiÃ³n y los Estados miembros. No me cansarÃ© de repetirlo: el Ã©xito de los fondos europeos Next Generation, asÃ­ como del resto de programas europeos, no dependerÃ¡ de los brillantes proyectos o ideas, sino de su rÃ¡pida ejecuciÃ³n, para que los recursos lleguen a la economÃ­a real. Para que tengan impacto.

Y, en este sentido, lamento tener que referirme a mi paÃ­s, a EspaÃ±a. EspaÃ±a es uno de los grandes beneficiarios de fondos europeos. Por ello, no podemos seguir asumiendo como normal lo que el Tribunal de Cuentas lleva denunciando durante aÃ±os, algo que vuelve a hacer en este informe: falta de transparencia y opacidad, tasas de ejecuciÃ³n ridÃ­culas, constantes incumplimientos y retrasos con lo pactado, un impacto de las inversiones y las reformas infinitamente menor de lo esperado... En definitiva, un fracaso.

Â¿Es de verdad este el modelo para el prÃ³ximo marco financiero? Lo dudo.

Â 
Â Â 
Â Â 

Interventions Ã  la demande

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Regina Doherty (PPE). â Mr President, I want to welcome the presentation of the European Court of Auditors 2024 Annual Report and thank Tony, my fellow Irishman and his colleagues, for the work. The overall error rate in EU spending has fallen from 5.6Â % to 3.2Â %, and that genuinely is progress. But it is still materially above the threshold of 2Â %, meaning there is a lot of work to do.

But when it comes to the RRF, I believe the Court of Auditors should be playing a much stronger role. The RRF is a real symbol of solidarity, but the court has identified gaps in transparency, cost verification and control consistency right across our Member States and this makes it difficult to show value for money and increases the risk of duplication with other EU funds.

So, for credibility of EU spending, I am looking for the Commissioner and the Commission to develop a harmonised framework for reporting and verifying the actual costs of RRF-funded measures, to establish a central EU registry, to track projects right across the EU institutions and prevent duplication of funding, to strengthen the ex-post audit and clawback mechanisms to ensure proper use of funds, and finally to publish a clear, accessible data-linking funding to tangible results that citizens can understand and trust.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â OndÅej Knotek (PfE). â MrÂ President, dear representatives of ECA,Â many thanks for the report. The expenditures from the EUÂ 2024 budget on the heading of natural resources and environment were almost EURÂ 65Â billion, which represents almost one third of the total expenditures.

I would like to have two questions. Has the Court identified cases where the Climate Pact investments did not deliver measurable environmental benefits? If so, how significant were these discrepancies relative to the total reported climate spending?

And the second question is on electro-mobility. To which extent did the Court elaborate and consider the life-cycle emissions of electric vehicles, including production and energy sourcing, within the audit scope, particularly when attributing emission reductions to electric vehicles' uptake?

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Grzegorz Braun (NI). â MrÂ President, hardly anybody is listening to this debate and that is probably why nobody is calling the cops â because this is about big money, probably the biggest money you can imagine in Europe.

The report that most honourable President Murphy has presented is actually partially a ready indictment for a prosecutor. President Murphy should be probably appointed to the European Union's bankruptcy trustee, which I would recommend because he himself is probably one of the most reliable clerks we have in this otherwise criminal organisation â because had that happened to some private business, some manager losing that much money, losing trace of that much money, that would be a case for ...

(The President cut off the speaker)

Â 
Â Â 
Â Â 

(Fin des interventions Ã  la demande)

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Piotr Serafin, Member of the Commission. â Mr President, honourable Members, thank you for sharing your views in this debate. As I have said already at the beginning, the Commission is committed to continuously improve its management of the EU budget and to implement the recommendations of the Court. The views of this House and the reports of the Court of Auditors were also key input for the Commission's proposal for the next MFF. Today's debate was actually concentrated not that much on the report of the Court, not that much about 2024, but about the future, which is, to an extent, also justified.

One of you said, 'We have to learn from our mistakes and not repeat them'. I could not agree more. Most of the comments were about the mistakes related to the RRF, and I will come back to it because, indeed, clarifications are needed in what extent the proposal of the Commission learns the lesson from the RRF mistakes. But we should not forget about, and the report of the Court is also about, other spending programmes, I should even say primarily about spending programmes other than the RRF. When you look into the report, that is what it is.

So what are those lessons that we should learn? The Court supports, but also our assessment identifies, that the areas with complex rules are those areas where the risk of errors is the highest. Incidentally, in those areas payments are also based on costs incurred by, and declared by, beneficiaries. We talk here about cohesion policy, we talk here about the eco-schemes under the common agricultural policy. Just two examples, but I think very much prominent also in the report of the Courts.

So what lesson should we learn? That is reflected in our proposal for the next MFF: we reduce the number of spending programmes â and that should be one of the key lessons. And let us take the example of the cohesion policy family. We have the Cohesion Fund, we have the European Regional Development Fund, we have European Social Fund Plus, we have the Just Transition Fund, we have the Modernisation Fund, we have Interreg, with separate legal bases, with different eligibility criteria, with different time frames of implementation. That is the reason for the errors. Therefore, we propose a single rule book and we propose the concentration of the spending programmes.

Now, RRF. Yes, we know that we need to learn the lessons from â and I will give you only a few examples â lack or insufficient transparency, that has been mentioned also in this debate. What do we do? We make sure that in the next MFF, information on beneficiaries, not only final beneficiaries â and as you know, the debate is still ongoing with the Member States on who is a final beneficiary â but also on recipients, final recipients, contractors and subcontractors,Â should be publicly available, both at the level of the Member States and also at the level of the Union. And transparency is the most powerful tool to ensure that the European taxpayers' money is spent properly.

Another criticism: insufficient protection of the EU's financial interests, again present in this debate. What do we propose for the future? We propose â which is missing in the current areas â that the state aid and public procurement rules should be part of the Member States' key requirements, and there, they should be subject to audits.

We are also aware, and that was present in today's debate, about the weaknesses of the delivery model. Weaknesses related primarily to the question of costs. Therefore, we propose also comprehensive costing review at midterm and confirmation by Member States at the end of the period that payments to Member States did not exceed payments to beneficiaries. The Commission will also have the right to propose amendments if we disagree with the assessment of the Member States.

We are also aware that there are weaknesses of the Member States' management and control systems. And actually, the model which we propose gives us a chance to, at the inception of the national plan, correct those deficiencies and to make meeting the 15 requirements defined in the regulation a prerequisite of entering into contractual obligation in the next multiannual financial framework.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Tony Murphy, President of the Court of Auditors. â MrÂ President, it's always interesting when an auditor and an auditee get together to discuss the findings.

I think it's normal that we don't always agree on everything, and I think there's nothing wrong with that. I think it should be remembered, though, as I think the Commissioner pointed out in his earlier presentation, that we have a common goal here, and the common goal is to improve the financial management and to protect the financial interests of the EU budget.

It is a collective effort, I think, based on our specific mandates. I am very happy that you say our recommendations are practically all accepted. That's good. It relates to one which was raised by MrÂ Erixon about the access to documents. We actually have a recommendation in Chapter 8 on migration, border management, security and defence about the access to documents which you have partly accepted.Â So there is something there.

But I really hope that you listen to our concerns on the RRF. I don't want to pre-empt their opinions, but based on your contribution here, I don't think we will still see eye to eye based on what you said. I have to say up front, because there's no point saying after the event when it's too late.

I think we're facing a very challenging period ahead. Look at the amount of money: we still have 40Â % of these RRF funds which have to be spent by AugustÂ 2026. We have an MFF in 21-27 where the absorption rate is 7Â % at the moment â 5Â % for cohesion. For us, based on our past experience, the worst thing you can do is put pressure on Member States to spend money by a certain deadline, because there is a distinct correlation between the errors we will find and that situation. I think that's to be avoided at all costs if it can be.

I want to take this opportunity to reiterate our concerns regarding the delivery and governance of the RRF, as well as the existing control mechanisms within the traditional budget. If these issues are not addressed, the transparency and traceability risks we have identified could significantly increase for the MFF. I can only recommend that you look at our RRF review, which we published on 6Â May. There's a summary page there. It's one page, and I think it outlines in simple language the key concerns and challenges we see with the RRF.

The Commissioner mentioned in his summary that we shouldn't focus too much on the RRF because it's not the substantial part of the budget in monetary terms. We're talking about EURÂ 200Â billion compared to maybe EURÂ 56Â billion for the RRF for last year. I think that's not the issue. Given the political fanfare which went with the RRF, the reputational risk with the RRF is huge for the EU project as a whole. I think it's not just a question of the actual proportional amount of money, it's about the expectations that have been generated in the EU and across the Member States.

I don't want to pre-empt. We're working on our opinions, which we will provide on the MFF proposals, and these will highlight the risks and lessons learned based on our extensive experience. Again, going back to reiterate, because I think it's very important what you said, the objective of these opinions is that we don't repeat the past mistakes. We're not in a crisis situation now, as was the case when the RRF was designed. So we have time to think about this and make sure that it's a better-designed regulation and a better-designed delivery mechanism.

As I say, we have a lot of concerns. I don't want to pre-empt our opinion on the national plans, but as I say, from what you say already, I'm not so convinced that all of the concerns that we have been addressed to our full satisfaction.

With that, I'd like to thank the Members here today who thanked the Court for the work. I mean, this work is not possible without the dedication of our staff. I would like to thank all the staff who were involved in this product and the reporting members and the college for producing a document which I think is high quality and I think is an important contribution to your work here and into the decision-making process, particularly given the circumstances we are in at the moment.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Le PrÃ©sident. â Le dÃ©bat est clos.

Â 

16. The need for a united support to Ukraine and for a just and durable peace concluded on Ukraine's terms, with Europeans and without surrendering to Vladimir Putin's conditions ahead of the foreseen Budapest summit (debate)
Video of the speeches
Â 

Â Â Le PrÃ©sident. â L'ordre du jour appelle Ã  prÃ©sent la dÃ©claration du Conseil et de la Commission sur la nÃ©cessitÃ© d'un soutien uni Ã  l'Ukraine et d'une paix juste et durable, conclue aux conditions de l'Ukraine avec les EuropÃ©ens et sans plier face aux conditions de Vladimir Poutine, en amont du sommet annoncÃ© Ã  Budapest (2025/2945(RSP)).

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Marie Bjerre, President-in-Office of the Council. â Mr President, honourable Members, Commissioner, over three and a half years ago, Russia started its brutal, unjustified and unprovoked full-scale invasion of Ukraine â a war of aggression that has had a terrible impact on Ukraine. Civilians suffer from air raids. Civilians are illegally detained by Russia. Ukrainian children are moved to Russia by force. And prisoners of war are tortured and inhumanely treated.

We welcome all efforts to achieve a just and lasting peace, and we support the US in its efforts. However, we have not yet seen any indications that Putin is generally ready for peace. On the contrary, Russia is escalating its war in Ukraine ahead of the winter season. We see more attacks against civilians and the energy infrastructure.

The Council has been clear: the EU stands united in its support of Ukraine's sovereignty and right to determine its own future. Without Ukraine, there cannot be negotiations about Ukraine. We must stay united and increase our support to Ukraine. This also includes diplomatic efforts, and we must continue to increase the pressure on Russia: economic pressure, military pressure, political pressure. The goal is that Russia engages truly in negotiations.

Sanctions are an essential part of the EU's policy to achieve this objective. This week we aim to adopt the 19th sanctions package. This package will target the Russian fossil fuels, the shadow fleet and the circumventions. And we already aim for a for next package. The diplomatic talks have confirmed over and over again that nobody wants peace more than the Ukrainians themselves. This peace should start with a full, unconditional and instant ceasefire at the first step. The Council has supported efforts for a ceasefire and leaders will discuss recent efforts at tomorrow's European Council meeting.

Last but not least, let me recall that the EU accession process is still the most important security guarantee that the EU can give to Ukraine. Accession is essential for Ukraine's stability, resilience and future security.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Jessika Roswall, Member of the Commission. â Mr President, Minister, honourable Members, it has now been three and a half years since the start of Russia's full-scale invasion. We must not forget that this war is not only about Ukraine's territorial integrity and freedom, it's about defending European security and the international rule based order that keeps our world stable and our people safe.

The fundamentals have not changed. Ukraine is ready for a ceasefire, but Russia shows no genuine interest in peace. We all support President Trump's efforts to end this war, but let us be clear. Putin will only negotiate when he understands that he is losing this war. The European Commission, together with all EU institutions and the Member States, has mobilised unprecedented support for Ukraine, especially because Ukraine needs our continued support on every front: military support to defend its people and territory, financial resilience to keep hospitals, schools and public services running, protection of the energy infrastructure constantly under Russian attack, humanitarian assistance to for millions of its citizens in need at home and across Europe, uninterrupted train through the solidarity lanes to stay connected to global markets.Â We are also supporting Ukraine to implement ambitious reforms towards EU membership, which also strengthen the country's institutional resilience.

From the very beginning, the European Commission acted swiftly, decisively, and in unity with our Member States. Together, we have provided EUR 177.5 billion of assistance, including EUR 63.2 billion of military support. Together, we are the largest donor, the largest public investor and the strongest partner in Ukraine's defence. With the coalition of the willing, we are shaping the EU's contribution to a robust and credible security guarantees, building on the 2024 joint security commitments.

Dear colleagues, we have come a long way in supporting Ukraine, yet we need to do more. Now is the time to accelerate our collective efforts to help Ukraine stand strong, not only to defend itself, but also to achieve just and lasting peace when hostilities give way to negotiations.

First, we must keep up our military support. We have two important missions on the ground: the EU Military Assistance Mission, that has already trained 8080 2000 Ukrainian soldiers, and the European Union's Advisory Mission, our civilian mission, that supports Ukraine, law enforcement and justice.Â We are also scaling up our defence industry cooperation with Ukraine.

Second, we must maintain our predictable long term financial assistance for Ukraine's defence, reconstruction and resilience. The new reparation loans proposed by the European Commission, funded from the cash balance associated with the immobilised Russian assets, is indispensable.

Third, we should make the most out of Ukraine facility to ensure micro and macro financial stability and support Ukraine's reforms.

And fourth, we must accelerate the EU accession process. Our most important security guarantee to Ukraine. Our commitment to stability, resilience and shared economic opportunity for Ukrainians as well as for European citizens. Ukraine has achieved remarkable reform progress in extremely challenging circumstances. And it is time to open negotiations on Cluster 1, the fundamentals for accession.

Our message is clear. We will stand with Ukraine for as long as it takes and for as long as it matters.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Michael Gahler, on behalf of the PPE Group. â MrÂ President, what has been said thus far by the Council and the Commission, I fully subscribe to. The large majority of this Parliament fully subscribes to it. It is indeed about a just and durable peace for Ukraine, as the title says, and nothing will be decided about Ukraine without Ukraine.

But while Putin has always had the same maximalist positions, which are all unacceptable and infamous, even when it comes to the alleged need for 'denazification' that he always talks about, I think the Nazis here in the House support him. It cannot be about the denazification of Ukraine, with a Jewish President at the top.

The other unfortunate thing is that on the American side we see an almost daily shift of positioning. That requires, finally, that we really also get our acts together when it comes to the peace project â not only the ongoing programmes, but there is talk about an alleged 12-point plan that is being worked on with Ukraine that is right and good â so we find a way forward to strengthen Ukraine's position for the time when it comes. Well, the American positioning changed so quickly that even our title with a reference to Budapest is outdated.

On the substance, it's right that there is no such thing. It was, as such, already an unfriendly act, be it initiated from the Russians or the Americans, to hold it in Budapest. They should have done it in in TÃ¼rkiye, in Istanbul, where we have had previous rounds of such talks.

But the real point is, in my mind, that we need to make it very clear that if the ceasefire is the first point where apparently the United States President is now back to this position, ceasefire first and then negotiations, it must be something that Ukraine can live with. And that means that we will never accept any legal transfer of Ukrainian territory to Russia. De facto, something can temporarily be accepted, but never in a position that we would legally agree to it.

Â 
Â Â 
Â 

Â Â Thijs Reuten, on behalf of the S&D Group. â Madam President, Commission, Council, dear colleagues, one phone call was enough for Trump to again change his mind. Thankfully, reality kicked in early.

Putin is still not interested in peace â surprise, surprise. Ukraine wants peace. We want peace. Real peace involves talks and compromises, yes.Â The US could play a critical role, but we can do without Witkoff's ignorance, incompetence and conflicts of interest.

When Putin thinks he is gaining ground and gets to keep it, he will continue. Putin will not de-escalate unless Europe and the United States firmly and decisively escalate support and pressure. So Europe must take the initiative as a credible, proactive force for peace and not as a background actor for a Trump-Putin tragedy.

My proposal: enable deep strikes, boost Ukraine's air defence now, adopt a reparations loan and fair talks framework, starting with a ceasefire but including real security guarantees, accountability, reparations, the children, the prisoners â not just territory. Act, Member States, act to defend Ukraine and Europe!

Â 
Â Â 
Â
